html:
AQA GCSE English Literature — Leave Taking — 20 Most Likely Questions to Come Up (2026)

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section A (Modern Texts). 30 marks + 4 marks AO4 (SPaG). Recommended writing time: 50 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context) / AO4 (SPaG). NO extract — questions are essay-stems only.

How this list was generated

This Q-bank was built using Sophicly's AQA-Prediction-Algorithm-the model in its new-spec adapted form (see `AQA-Prediction-Algorithm-the model.md` + `AQA-Prediction-Algorithm-an earlier model iteration.md §5` + `AQA-Prediction-Algorithm-Leave-Taking.md` in this folder). Leave Taking is a new-spec text — first taught from September 2023, first live exam summer 2025 — so only one year of past-paper data exists. The standard the model formula (Frequency × 0.35 + CycleGap × 0.25 + CoverageGap × 0.15 + unexplored-angle signal × 0.15 + core-topic boost × 0.20 − ExactAngleRepeatPenalty × 0.10) cannot run on a one-year dataset, so the algorithm substitutes the an earlier model iteration §5 sparse-data signals: SpecAOWeight × 0.30 + SpecimenPaperMatch × 0.35 + AnalogousTextPattern × 0.15 + SiblingBoardSignal × 0.10 + core-topic boost × 0.10. AQA has not published a verifiable specimen-paper question for Leave Taking, so SpecimenPaperMatch scores zero across the board. Eduqas also introduced Leave Taking in 2025 (replacing A Taste of Honey), so SiblingBoardSignal effectively scores zero as well. The two active signals are therefore the spec's named AOs and the analogous-text patterns from AQA's established Section A modern-text siblings — An Inspector Calls, Blood Brothers, Animal Farm, Lord of the Flies, and Anita and Me. The the model spine boost still applies: three core themes (Caribbean inheritance / Mother-daughter / Belonging-displacement) and three core characters (Enid / Del / Mai) each receive `core-topic boost = 1.0`. The the model fix that mattered for the 2026 Macbeth = Guilt and R&J = Conflict actuals also applies here: ExactAngleRepeatPenalty fires only at the same angle asked in 2025, not at the person or theme as a whole. The 2025 stems were Enid-as-strong-mother and difficult-older-younger relationships, so a fresh-angle Enid Q (Windrush experience rather than matriarch) and a fresh-angle generational Q (cross-generational inheritance rather than older-younger conflict) remain live candidates under the model and are restored to the top set. The Coverage Pack appendix carries the exact 2025 angles for canonical coverage only.

Note on section labels. Section IDs (C1–C10 + T1–T10) are stable character/theme IDs used throughout the cross-reference table at the foot of the document; they are NOT priority order. The priority order under the model (substitute method) is given in the tables below.

Priority order — rehearse in this order

Characters (C1–C10 in priority order):

| Section | | Character | Confidence | Why |

|---:|---|---|---|---|

| 1 | C1 | Del | MEDIUM-HIGH | Spine character + protagonist coming-of-age. Sibling pattern: AIC-Sheila pattern (Spec + 2019 + 2025) confirms AQA returns to a new text's protagonist in years 1–3. Not asked 2025. |

| 2 | C2 | Enid (Windrush-experience angle — NOT strong-mother) | MEDIUM-HIGH | Spine character. ExactAngleRepeatPenalty avoided by switching from the 2025 strong-mother angle to the Windrush-experience angle. Run at Windrush-experience angle (NOT matriarchy framing — that is the 2025 angle, still valid as secondary). |

| 3 | C3 | Mai | MEDIUM | Spine character + structural-Eva archetype (off-stage-vital mentor never asked). Sibling pattern: Inspector / Old Major / Nanima all rotate in years 2–3 of a new text. |

| 4 | C4 | Brod | MEDIUM | Boxer-2021 archetype (broken working-class male figure). Sibling pattern: AF 2021 directly precedented. |

| 5 | C5 | The female line — Enid, Mai and Del across three generations | MEDIUM | Two core characters + structural female-line argument. Sibling pattern: AIC 2023 generational frame inverted as character study. |

| 6 | C6 | Enid and Brod as the Windrush generation (multi-char) | MEDIUM | Spine character + Windrush pair. Bypasses the 2025 Enid solo recency penalty by pairing. Sibling pattern: AIC 2025 Sheila + Gerald multi-char precedent. |

| 7 | C7 | Mai and Enid as chosen and biological mother (multi-char) | MEDIUM-LOW | Two core characters in pair. Tests the extended-kinship structure of the diasporic household. |

| 8 | C8 | Enid and Del's relationship (multi-char) | MEDIUM-LOW | Two core characters. Recency-bordering: Enid solo at strong-mother asked 2025, but the relationship framing is distinct and Del-anchored. |

| 9 | C9 | Del and Viv as sisters (multi-char) | LOW-MEDIUM | Multi-char Q following AIC-2025 Sheila + Gerald precedent. Two-route response of the second generation. |

| 10 | C10 | Viv | LOW-MEDIUM | Studious-sibling-foil archetype (Eric precedent). Less stage time than Del, harder for examiner to set without an extract. |

Themes (T1–T10 in priority order):

| Section | | Theme | Confidence | Why |

|---:|---|---|---|---|

| 1 | T1 | Identity, Britishness and Caribbean inheritance | MEDIUM-HIGH | Spine theme + spec's flagship AO3 anchor + never asked. AQA's Spark Conversation materials name identity as the play's headline theme. |

| 2 | T2 | Belonging and displacement | MEDIUM-HIGH | Spine theme (belonging-displacement) + fresh angle distinct from Identity Q. The play's title-anchored question staged as the territory between two homelands. |

| 3 | T3 | The mother-daughter relationship | MEDIUM | Spine theme + sibling pattern: BB Mothers-and-Sons, Anita Mother-Daughter, AIC 2023 women. Distinct from the 2025 older-younger framing. |

| 4 | T4 | Cultural inheritance | MEDIUM | Spine theme (Caribbean inheritance) at the structural rather than identity-naming angle. Handwritten book / patois / Obeah practice as inheritance forms. |

| 5 | T5 | Coming-of-age and change | MEDIUM | Sibling pattern: AIC-Sheila / Eric change angle asked 5× in 9 years; LotF loss-of-innocence; Anita coming-of-age. Distinct from 2025 generational framing. |

| 6 | T6 | Race and racism in 1980s Britain | MEDIUM | Sibling pattern: Anita 2023 directly precedented. LT has the anchor moments (Gullyman, Viv's workplace, legislative chain). |

| 7 | T7 | Memory, Windrush legacy and leave-taking | MEDIUM-LOW | Title-anchored theme. Strong AO3 hooks (1948 BNA, 1962 CIA, 2018 Windrush scandal). No direct sibling precedent. |

| 8 | T8 | Religion and spiritual inheritance | MEDIUM-LOW | Spec-named, genuinely LT-specific (no sibling precedent reduces AnalogousTextPattern). Clean AO3 anchors (Obeah Act 1898, Caribbean cosmology). |

| 9 | T9 | Family, household and the secrets the host country imposed | LOW-MEDIUM | Combines family/household frame with truth-telling. Sibling pattern: AIC family / paternal-authority gap. |

| 10 | T10 | Generational change and cross-generational inheritance (fresh angle — NOT older-younger conflict) | LOW-MEDIUM | Spine-adjacent + fresh angle on the 2025-asked generational topic. ExactAngleRepeatPenalty avoided by switching from older-younger conflict to cross-generational inheritance. |

Coverage Pack (rehearse but not top-20 under the model): Enid at the 2025 strong-mother angle, Generational at the 2025 older-younger conflict angle, Truth-telling as a standalone Q. These angles were asked recently. Rehearse them as canonical coverage and as secondary 2026 predictions — AQA's cycle suggests fresh angles are more likely but recently-asked angles can still come up.

How to use this document

Each question prints a typical AQA stem, a one-paragraph frame, a short technique gloss, and a skeleton plan in Sophicly's locked structure (Introduction 3 sentences / three Body Paragraphs of 7 sentences / Conclusion 4 sentences). If exam time is tight, rehearse the top 5 of each axis first (rows 1–5 in the tables above). For comprehensive coverage, rehearse all 20. Memorise the cross-question scene-reuse table at the end so the same scene-bank answers every Q here.

Pedagogical depth — three non-negotiables for HIGH-confidence picks

Every Grade-9 essay on Leave Taking should thread at least one of these readings into one BP or the conclusion. They are the play's deepest political moves; Sophia teaches them once so students can deploy them across any Q.

1. Enid as strong-mother archetype OR Enid as the Windrush MOTHER'S COST — the play's deepest counter-reading.

The standard reading of Enid celebrates her as the strong-mother archetype the diaspora's first generation requires: two jobs, breadwinner authority, household held together against the host country's racism. The 2025 AQA Q at the strong-mother angle confirms how readily examiners and students settle into this frame. The sharper counter-reading refuses the celebration and reads Enid inside Pinnock's critique. Enid's two jobs, her silence about Mai-in-Jamaica, her broken-speech confession at Mai's bedsit ("I have to be mother, father, breadwinner") name the WINDRUSH MOTHER'S COST, not her strength. The two jobs are extraction, not strength. The silence is wound, not authority. The composure she presents to Del and Viv is the labour of a woman whose own mother has been kept off-stage for eighteen years to protect the household from the truth of what migration extracted from the mother who travelled. Pinnock's deepest move is exposing what migration extracts from mothers, not what mothers contribute. The play's pedagogical pivot is from the celebratory frame (the host country's preferred image of the resilient Windrush mother) to the structural frame (the host country's invisible labour-cost). Read this way, Enid's Scene 6 broken-speech is not strength rendered articulate but cost rendered articulate; the cleaned bedsit in Scene 8 is the cost being inherited rather than the strength being transmitted. The handwritten book Del receives in Scene 8 carries both the inheritance and the record of what was paid for it. Thread this into: C2 Enid / C5 Female line / C6 Enid and Brod / C7 Mai and Enid / T2 Belonging and displacement / T3 Mother-daughter / T7 Memory and Windrush legacy / any conclusion that names the play's authorial position on the diasporic mother.

2. The Caribbean-British diaspora double consciousness — Enid's silence about Mai-in-Jamaica replicating the silence colonial Britain imposed on the Windrush generation.

Enid leaves her mother — Mai-in-Jamaica, the off-stage matriarch whose death by telephone in Scene 3 is the play's structural pivot — and across the eight scenes refuses to articulate the cost of that leaving. The silence is doubled. Privately, Enid has carried for eighteen years a suppressed truth about her own mother's reaction to her departure, the kind of silence that holds a household together at the cost of healing it. Publicly, that silence replicates the silence colonial Britain imposed on the Windrush generation: a Britain that recruited Caribbean labour between 1948 and 1971, then progressively erased the documentation of their right to remain (1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, 1971 Immigration Act, 1981 British Nationality Act, culminating in the 2018 Windrush deportation scandal). Del's reception arc — Scene 1 mockery, Scene 7 named gift, Scene 8 cleaned bedsit and palm-reading — is Pinnock's argument that silenced inheritance must be excavated to be claimed. Stuart Hall's Cultural Identity and Diaspora (1990) names diasporic identity as "a matter of 'becoming' as well as of 'being'"; Del's becoming is the play's structural answer to Britain's institutional forgetting. Thread this into: C1 Del / C2 Enid / C5 Female line / T1 Identity / T2 Belonging / T3 Mother-daughter / T7 Memory and Windrush legacy / any essay with a Scene 8 BP.

3. Mai's spiritual practice as alternative epistemology, NOT exotic folklore — recovery as cosmology, not curiosity.

Pinnock stages two cosmologies inside the play with equal naturalistic weight: the pastor's Scene 2 visit to Enid's living room and Mai's Obeah practice across Scenes 1, 5, 6, 7 and 8. She declines to fold one inside the other. The pastor is not framed as primary cosmology against Mai's secondary folklore — they are doubled, and Pinnock's structural refusal to subordinate Mai is itself the argument. Obeah is the Afro-Caribbean spiritual tradition that carries African religious memory through enslavement and migration; criminalised in Jamaica under the Obeah Act of 1898 (the statute remains on the books to this day) and treated by colonial Britain as superstition to be eradicated. Pinnock presents Mai's practice as an alternative epistemology — a complete system of knowing, healing and inheriting that imperial Britain attempted to erase. Mai's clairvoyant reading, Enid's consultation in Scene 6 ("I have to be mother, father, breadwinner"), Mai's recognition of Del's "gift of the Obeah" in Scene 7, and the closing palm-reading in Scene 8 register the epistemology working at the level of the household. The counter-reading exposes the host country's framing of Obeah as exotic spiritual practice as itself the imperial gesture — what colonial Britain attempted to erase by classification, Pinnock restores by structural equivalence with the pastor's visit. Recovering Mai's practice is therefore not the recovery of a relative but the recovery of a cosmology — an alternative system of knowing the British schooling that produced Del and Viv had taught them to refuse. The handwritten book transferred to Del in Scene 8 is body-to-body transmission of a knowledge system the host country had refused to recognise. Thread this into: C3 Mai / C1 Del / T1 Identity / T4 Cultural inheritance / T8 Religion / any essay with a Scene 8 BP.

Why this depth matters pedagogically. AQA's top-band descriptor asks for "a critical, exploratory, conceptualised response". A reading that locates the play's argument at the level of imperial epistemology and post-colonial inheritance — that names the Windrush MOTHER'S COST rather than celebrating the strong-mother archetype — and that traces the formal mechanism (eight-scene cyclical structure, Mai's bedsit at open and close, the handwritten book as material vehicle) is the species of conceptualisation Examiner Reports name as Grade 9. Without it, a Del essay is sympathetic coming-of-age; with it, the essay is a post-colonial argument about inheritance traced to its dramatic device.

A universal critical-angle method. Every text in the AQA spec rewards the same move: identify the standard reading; then identify a counter-reading the same evidence supports; then read the two together as the species of conceptualisation Grade 9 demands. For Leave Taking the dual readings are strong-mother/Windrush MOTHER'S COST (Enid), exotic-folklore/alternative-cosmology (Mai's Obeah practice), individual-becoming/structural-inheritance (Del). Sophia should generate the second reading whenever helping a student plan — the standard reading is what most students will write; the second reading is what carries them past the band-9 line.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape

1. Topic sentence — concept only, no techniques named.

2. Technique + evidence — one quote with multiple rhetorical techniques visible inside it.

3. Close analysis — grammar, word-class, syntax, and one further observation about the same quote.

4. Effect on audience 1 — what the audience sees, hears, feels.

5. Effect on audience 2 — what the audience recognises, registers, infers.

6. Author's purpose — what Pinnock may be arguing through this scene.

7. Context (AO3) — one period-specific fact that lands the BP back at the controlling concept.

Memorise these once

Leave Taking argues that the Caribbean diaspora's second generation inherits a mother's exile without inheriting her homeland — that silenced Caribbean inheritance must be claimed across three generations or lost — and that the cost of leave-taking is paid not by the generation that left but by the generation that was never asked. Pinnock writes for two audiences at once: the Windrush mothers who must break the silences the host country imposed on them, and the British-born daughters who must claim the inheritance the host country taught them to refuse. The play is set in 1987 against the recent backdrop of Thatcher Britain's 1981 British Nationality Act and the cumulative hostile-environment legislation (1962 CIA / 1971 IA / 1981 BNA) that progressively eroded the Windrush generation's right to remain. The closing image — Del reading her mother's palm inside Mai's cleaned bedsit — is the controlling concept staged as one quiet physical action: healing across the diasporic mother-daughter line requires the trained daughter to make a room inside which the mother's silenced truths can finally be spoken.

Key terms:

• Diaspora — a people dispersed from their homeland; here, the Caribbean migration to Britain after 1948 and the family it produced inside Enid's North London living room.

• Patois — Jamaican English variety with its own grammar and phonology; the speech of the Windrush generation in the play (Enid, Brod, Mai), set against the south-east London speech of Del and Viv.

• Obeah — the Afro-Caribbean spiritual tradition carrying African religious memory through enslavement and migration; criminalised in Jamaica under the Obeah Act of 1898; treated by Pinnock with the same naturalistic seriousness she gives the pastor's visit.

• Naturalism — late-nineteenth-century dramatic tradition (Ibsen, Chekhov) in which character is the product of social conditions and dialogue carries the action; Pinnock extends it for Black British theatre.

• Episodic structure — a play built of multiple discrete scenes (eight here) rather than a tightly continuous three-act arc; allows Del's pregnancy to advance, a grandmother to die off-stage, a household to drift apart at the pace of family life.

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — the heightened device (Mai's Obeah readings, the handwritten book, the closing palm-reading) Pinnock holds inside naturalistic staging so the inheritance is taken seriously rather than folklorised.

Three context factors that travel across all 20 questions (full version at the foot of the document):

1. The Windrush Era + 1948 British Nationality Act. The Windrush generation arrived 1948–1971 carrying British passports under the 1948 BNA. "Most Caribbean migrants arrived on British-subject passports the 1962 Act retrospectively voided — that documentary erasure became the 2018 Windrush scandal." The 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act introduced employment vouchers; the 1971 Immigration Act made "patriality" the test for unrestricted entry; the 1981 British Nationality Act stripped the automatic citizenship rights the 1948 Act had granted.

2. 1981 Britain. The 1981 British Nationality Act passed in the same year as the Brixton uprising (10–12 April), the Toxteth riots (July), and the New Cross fire (18 January, in which thirteen young Black people died). The play premiered 11 November 1987 inside the cultural moment those events forced into public visibility — the formation of Black British identity as a distinct political and aesthetic category.

3. Obeah as suppressed Caribbean cosmology. Criminalised under the Jamaican Obeah Act 1898 (imprisonment with hard labour for practitioners; the statute remains on the Jamaican books to this day). The practice survived through clandestine transmission across enslavement and migration. Pinnock has framed Obeah in interview as "traces of African spiritual rituals preserved by clandestine practices" — a religious inheritance carrying the memory of an African heritage that enslavement attempted to erase.

Top 10 Character Questions

Most likely AQA Modern Text character questions, ordered by examiner-prediction confidence. Each carries one ready-to-rehearse plan with seven-sentence body paragraphs and an empty response area to draft against.

C1 — Del

Question

How does Pinnock present Del and her change in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Del at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses Del's change to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Del is Pinnock's most complete dramatisation of the change the diaspora's second generation must undergo — a young woman who travels from defensive mockery, through painful recognition, to trained reception of the inheritance she had refused. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 stolen charm, Scene 7 kicked wall and named gift, Scene 8 cleaned bedsit and palm-reading.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Caribbean-British double consciousness reading (see top of document). Del's reception arc dramatises the play's argument that silenced inheritance must be excavated to be claimed. Land this in BP3 or the conclusion at minimum.

Technique gloss:

• Stage business — wordless physical action carrying meaning (the stolen charm; the kicked wall; the cleaned bedsit).

• Recurring object — an object that returns across scenes with cumulative meaning (Mai's handwritten book of Obeah practice).

• Anagnorisis — Aristotelian moment of recognition; Mai's "Del may have the gift of the Obeah" names Del's inheritance for her.

• Coming-of-age / Bildungsroman — the protagonist moves from innocence through experience to maturity.

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — the heightened device (Obeah practice) Pinnock holds inside naturalism.

• Cyclical structure — the play's Scene 8 returns to Mai's bedsit (the Scene 1 setting), now cleaned and inherited.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Pinnock won the George Devine Award in 1991 for Leave Taking — the first Black British woman to win it — and the prize honours the Royal Court tradition of new writing whose protagonists travel furthest in shortest stage time, which is exactly the journey Pinnock builds for Del across eight scenes.

• Building sentence (historical context): Del belongs to the British-born second generation — the daughters of Windrush whose schooling was English Literature, whose workplace colleagues treat them as if they do not "speak English", and whose 1981 Britain framed Black-British identity for the first time as a distinct political and cultural category through the New Cross fire and the Brixton uprising — and Pinnock constructs her arc as the test case of whether that generation can claim what the host country taught them to refuse.

• Thesis: Across her Scene 1 mockery of Mai's Obeah practice and her stolen charm in which the inheritance is refused, her Scene 7 kicked wall and Mai's recognition of "the gift of the Obeah" in which the inheritance is named, and her Scene 8 cleaning of Mai's bedsit, receipt of the handwritten book, and palm-reading of her own mother in which the inheritance is finally received, Pinnock presents Del as the figure through whom the diasporic second generation's coming-of-age becomes possible.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 1): defensive mockery and stolen charm (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Mai's Deptford bedsit; Mai conducts a clairvoyant reading for Enid; Del watches with hostile resistance and steals a charm from Mai's table as the consultation closes.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock initially presents Del as a daughter defending herself against the inheritance her mother carries — the British-born second generation whose first instinct is to refuse what looks foreign in a country that has trained her to want only what looks British.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys stage business inside dismissive register — Del's stealing of a charm from Mai's table at the close of the consultation, framed by Del's verbal "waste of time" dismissal of the reading itself — combining stage business (the wordless theft as the physical form of the refusal Del has not yet articulated) with dismissive register (the colloquial idiom "waste of time" drawn from the south-east London speech of Del's generation, set against the patois of the older women in the room) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The simultaneity of the verbal dismissal and the physical theft is the play's first quiet contradiction — Del has called the practice worthless, yet her hand has reached for one of its objects, the body acknowledging what the speech denies.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels Del's refusal as itself a form of attention — the daughter who steals a charm has, at minimum, recognised which object on Mai's table is the one worth taking.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the dangerous confidence of assimilation — the way a British-born daughter can call her mother's cultural inheritance "superstition" in the very act of betraying that she has registered its meaning.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be establishing that the inheritance Del will eventually receive has been visible to her from the opening scene, and that the journey she will take across the eight scenes is the journey from refused recognition to claimed reception.

• Context (AO3): In 1987, second-generation Black British identity was being formed against the host country's casual racism — the school that failed to diagnose Del's dyslexia, the workplace that treated her as if she did not "speak English" — and Del's stolen charm registers the cost of forming an identity in those conditions.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 7): kicked wall and named gift (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Brod has just told Del the buried truth about Viv's father — "originally a loving and hard-working man who was left broken and 'empty' by the way he was treated in Britain"; Del kicks the wall; Mai recognises what the kicked wall registers.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed Del's stance as defensive refusal, Scene 7 stages her painful recognition — the daughter who had read her mother's strictness as hatred now receives the explanation she had lacked, and the older woman in the room names the inheritance the daughter has been carrying without knowing it.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys stage business inside Mai's anagnorisis — Del's kicked wall in response to Brod's disclosure, immediately followed by Mai's "Del may have the gift of the Obeah" (Scene 7) — combining stage business (the kicked wall as the physical form of a recognition that exceeds language, the body absorbing what the speech cannot yet metabolise) with anagnorisis (Mai's recognition naming Del's inheritance for her, the older woman registering in the body's reaction what the daughter herself has not yet named) inside the same exchange.

• Close analysis: The sequencing is the argument — disclosure produces the kicked wall, the kicked wall produces Mai's recognition, the recognition produces the journey toward reception — three beats compressed into one stage moment, the play's structural hinge.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the kicked wall as a release of eighteen years of Del's misreading her mother — the absent father at the centre of her childhood now explained as a man broken by Britain rather than as a man Enid drove away.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Mai's naming of "the gift" as the moment the play turns — inheritance has to be recognised by an older practitioner before it can be received by a younger one, and the recognition is what permits the reception that follows in Scene 8.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be arguing that the diasporic second generation's coming-of-age depends on two disclosures simultaneously — the truth about the absent father (Brod's role) and the recognition of the inheritance the daughter has been carrying (Mai's role) — and that neither alone is sufficient.

• Context (AO3): The 1981 Brixton uprising and the New Cross fire of January 1981 named publicly what 1980s Black-British men in Britain were already living — the housing discrimination, the policing pressure, the workplace racism producing outcomes that broke marriages and households — and Brod's disclosure about Viv's father is one such outcome made domestic.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): cleaned bedsit and palm-reading (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit, now cleaned by Del; Mai passes the handwritten book of Obeah practice to Del; Enid arrives as Del's first client; Del reads her mother's palm.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 7 had named the inheritance, Scene 8 stages its full reception — Del now trained, the room now cleaned, the handwritten book now hers, and her mother seated in front of her as the first client of a practice the daughter had once mocked.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys cyclical structure inside the recurring object — the same Mai's bedsit that opened the play in Scene 1 "messy" now appears cleaned by Del, with Mai's "handwritten book" of Obeah practice transferred into Del's hands and the closing palm-reading staging Enid as Del's first client — combining cyclical structure (the unchanged room measuring the changed daughter, what looked like the end of a tradition in Scene 1 looking by Scene 8 like the survival of one) with recurring object (the handwritten book as cultural memory in material form, body-to-body transmission of what enslavement, colonial criminalisation, and migration had tried to erase) inside the same closing image.

• Close analysis: The unchanged room is the play's quietest argument — cultural inheritance survives in rooms like Mai's bedsit when there is somebody willing to receive it — while the handwritten quality of the book insists that the transmission is body-to-body rather than abstract, the older generation's hand to the younger generation's hand.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the closing image — a daughter's hand on a mother's hand, the inherited practice as the medium of touch — as a healing the play has earned across its full eight scenes rather than asserted in its closing minutes.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Del's journey as the journey Pinnock wants the audience itself to take — from a default position that treats Caribbean spiritual practice as foreign superstition to a position of recognising it as serious cultural inheritance worth claiming.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be making her clearest argument here — that healing across the diasporic mother-daughter line requires the trained daughter to make a room (Mai's cleaned bedsit, Del's reading hand) inside which the mother's silenced truths can finally be spoken aloud.

• Context (AO3): Obeah was criminalised in Jamaica under the 1898 Obeah Act — the statute remains on the Jamaican books to this day — and the practice survived through clandestine transmission across the diaspora; Del's receipt of Mai's handwritten book is one such transmission staged.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock traces Del's journey from defensive mockery, through painful recognition, to trained reception — the diasporic second generation's coming-of-age in dramatic miniature.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as refused inheritance, Scene 7 named as recognised inheritance, Scene 8 confirmed as received inheritance — three stages of the same arc, the consistency of the journey across the eight scenes confirming that change in the second generation is real and that the cost of leave-taking can begin to be paid back.

• Author's purpose: Writing for a 1987 audience inside the cultural emergence of Black British identity, Pinnock embodies in Del her conviction that the second generation can claim what the host country taught them to refuse, and that the claiming is the precondition for healing across the female line.

• Ultimate moral: Del's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that her arc is the audience's arc — the daughter who once mocked the inheritance has, by the close, become the room inside which the mother can finally speak.

C2 — Enid (Windrush-experience angle, NOT strong-mother)

Question

How does Pinnock use Enid to explore the experience of the Windrush generation in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Enid

- how Pinnock uses Enid to explore the experience of the Windrush generation.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Enid is Pinnock's portrait of the Windrush mother who carries a homeland she cannot return to, performs two jobs to give her British-born daughters a future she will not share, and finally breaks the eighteen-year silence that has kept her household held together but unhealed. The plan tracks three stages of the same diagnosis — Scene 1 patois discipline, Scene 4 grieving nostalgia after her mother's death in Jamaica, Scene 8 broken-speech confession at Mai's bedsit. Required the model framing. This Q runs Enid at the Windrush-experience angle (the legislative chain, the two jobs, the unfinished mourning, the cracked silence) — NOT at the 2025 strong-mother / matriarch angle, which the ExactAngleRepeatPenalty rules out. Avoid matriarchy framing within this Q only — that is the 2025 angle, which is in the Coverage Pack as a secondary option.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Caribbean-British double consciousness reading (see top of document). Enid's silence about her own mother in Jamaica replicates colonial Britain's silence about the Windrush generation; Scene 4 stages that doubled silence and Scene 8 begins to crack it. Thread into BP2 or BP3 at minimum.

Technique gloss:

• Jamaican patois — the speech the Windrush generation brought; phoneme-level evidence of inheritance.

• Asyndetic list — list without conjunctions ("Roast breadfruit. Callaloo").

• Broken speech / fragmented dialogue — Pinter-derived naturalistic device for suppressed truth surfacing.

• Stage direction — non-spoken authorial instruction; Mai's bedsit as Enid's only confessional space.

• Motif — recurring image carrying thematic weight; food, weariness, the telephone from Jamaica.

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses ("You tink dis is Jamaica? You tink you can…").

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock wrote Leave Taking at twenty-three, in the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, drawing on the North London household founded by her own Jamaican parents who arrived in the Windrush migration — and Pinnock said in 2018 that she made Enid the heroine because she had never seen a Caribbean-born hospital cleaner as the lead in a British play.

• Building sentence (historical context): Enid belongs to the generation Britain recruited between 1948 and 1971 to fill post-war labour shortages and then declined to make space for, and Pinnock places her inside a cramped North London living room across two jobs and three decades of holding a household together while the legislative chain of the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act and the 1971 Immigration Act progressively eroded her right to belong.

• Thesis: Across her Scene 1 patois discipline of Del in which Enid's authority is delivered in a homeland-register her British-born daughter does not share, her Scene 4 grieving nostalgia after her own mother's death in Jamaica in which the food motif carries an inheritance she cannot return to receive, and her Scene 8 broken-speech confession at Mai's bedsit in which the eighteen-year silence finally cracks, Pinnock presents Enid as the figure through whom the cost of the Windrush leave-taking becomes visible across the female line.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 1): patois authority and the homeland inside the host country (7 sentences — about 225 words)

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Enid's confrontation with Del over Del's late-night absences; the homeland's register set against the host country's register.

• Topic sentence: In the play's opening, Pinnock establishes Enid as a Windrush mother whose authority is delivered in a homeland-register her British-born daughter does not fully share, dramatising the diasporic household as a translation problem before it is a moral one.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys Jamaican patois inside anaphora — Enid's "You tink dis is Jamaica? You tink you can jus' walk outta mi 'ouse and come back any time you feel like?" (Scene 1) — combining patois (the homeland phoneme "tink" for "think", the demonstrative "dis" for "this", the contracted "mi 'ouse" for "my house" refusing the host country's standard English) with anaphora ("You tink… You tink…" twice opening successive interrogatives, the structural repetition mirroring a mother's exhausted reach for control) inside the same outburst.

• Close analysis: The repeated demonstrative "dis" insists on a Jamaica that is no longer geographically reachable but linguistically present in every clause Enid speaks, while the contraction "mi 'ouse" compresses possession into a homeland-grammar that the British-born daughter has never had to operate inside.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the Windrush experience in the sounds of the conversation before they hear it in the argument's content — patois on one side, south-east London speech on the other, the diaspora staged in phonemes.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that Enid's discipline is being delivered in a register Del has never lived inside, and registers that the friction is the texture of a household built on a migration neither daughter remembers.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the patois-versus-London-speech contrast to argue that the Windrush experience does not stay in the past for the generation that lived it — it is carried in the body and in the grammar of every clause the older generation speaks, and the friction with the host country is daily rather than historical.

• Context (AO3): Enid belongs to the Windrush generation (1948–1971) whom Britain recruited to fill post-war labour shortages — particularly in the new National Health Service founded in July 1948 — and then declined to make space for; Pinnock writes the older generation from inside her own family's experience.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 4): grieving the off-stage homeland (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Enid grieving on the sofa with Viv after the telephone has carried news of her mother's death in Jamaica; the disclosure of the saved university money.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed Enid's authority as patois discipline, Scene 4 strips that authority to its underlying cost — a Windrush mother who has worked two jobs to fund a future she cannot share, mourning a homeland mother she cannot return to bury, the legislative chain made personal.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys an asyndetic list inside food-motif and broken syntax — Enid's "Old time woman could cook. Roast breadfruit. Callaloo" (Scene 4) — combining asyndeton (the three Jamaican dishes set down without conjunctions, each item left alone like an unfinished sentence) with food-motif (each dish a material trace of Jamaica inside the London living room, callaloo and breadfruit standing for the cultural memory the host country has not made room for) inside the same nostalgic eruption.

• Close analysis: The asyndetic refusal to join the items with "and" leaves each Jamaican dish standing alone like an unfinished sentence, and the unfinished syntax mirrors the unfinished mourning — a daughter who could not travel back for her own mother's funeral can only assemble the homeland in fragments of remembered food.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the cost of the leave-taking Enid performed twenty years earlier as the inability to attend her own mother's funeral — grief delivered not through extended monologue but through a list of three dishes too short to carry it.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the saved university money for Viv is the diasporic version of the homeland inheritance Enid herself cannot bring back — transferred from mother to daughter as the only kind of cultural memory the host country has left available.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the grieving scene to argue that the Windrush experience is the experience of an inheritance redirected — from Jamaica to London, from breadfruit and callaloo to a university place — and that the redirection is what the leave-taking actually cost.

• Context (AO3): Most Caribbean migrants arrived on British-subject passports the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act retrospectively voided — that documentary erasure became the 2018 Windrush scandal — and by the 1980s the legislative chain of 1962, 1971 and 1981 had made return travel for the Windrush generation legally and economically harder than it had been for the original arrivals; Enid's absence from her mother's funeral is the legislative chain made personal.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): broken-speech confession at the cleaned bedsit (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Enid arrives at Mai's cleaned bedsit as Del's first Obeah client; the eighteen-year silence about her own marriage and her own mother's reaction to her departure from Jamaica finally begins to crack.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 4 had revealed the cost of the leave-taking, Scene 8 stages its possible repair — the Windrush mother finally permitted, by her trained daughter, to articulate the suppressed truths the host country had imposed on her.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys broken speech inside a stage-direction setting — Enid's halting Scene 8 confession to Del at Mai's cleaned bedsit, her sentences fragmented by the "overwhelming emotions" she has not spoken in eighteen years — combining broken speech (the unfinished clauses, the pauses, the restarts as the Pinter-derived form of cultural and personal silence beginning to break) with structural setting (the confession delivered inside Mai's room rather than Enid's living room, the cultural-inheritance space rather than the family-assimilation space) inside the same closing exchange.

• Close analysis: The fragmentation of Enid's speech is the form of the truth's difficulty — a woman who has not spoken these sentences for eighteen years cannot now speak them whole — while the setting transfers the disclosure into the cultural-inheritance space, registering that the truth can only be received in a room the older generation built.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the speech-fragmentation as the sound of a Windrush mother finally permitted, by her trained daughter, to articulate what eighteen years of host-country suppression had kept unsaid.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience receives Del's subsequent palm-reading of Enid as the play's quietest reversal — the daughter who once mocked Mai's practice now using it as the room inside which her Windrush mother can speak.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be addressing her 1987 audience on two registers at once — to the first-generation Windrush mothers in the auditorium, that the silences must be broken before the inheritance can be received; to the second-generation daughters, that the inheritance must be received before the silences can be broken.

• Context (AO3): Leave Taking premiered at the Liverpool Playhouse Studio on 11 November 1987 during the cultural emergence of Black British identity in the wake of the New Cross fire (1981) and the Brixton uprising (1981), and the play's 2018 Bush Theatre revival — opening weeks after the Guardian's reporting on the Windrush deportation scandal — confirmed Enid's Scene 8 silence as prescient.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock constructs Enid as a comprehensive portrait of the Windrush experience — patois discipline in Scene 1, grieving nostalgia and redirected inheritance in Scene 4, broken-speech confession in Scene 8 — making visible the cost the legislative chain and the cultural amnesia of the host country combined to extract.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as a translation problem, Scene 4 named as the unfinished mourning of a homeland that could not be returned to, Scene 8 began to repair through the daughter's trained reception — three stages of the same diagnosis, the doubled coming-of-age confirming that healing across the female line requires both halves of the household to move.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1987 inside the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, Pinnock honoured a Windrush hospital cleaner who had never been the lead in a British play before, refusing the melodrama of either saint or martyr and giving the audience an ordinary woman whose two jobs and unspoken truths were the indictment.

• Ultimate moral: Enid's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that the truth she has carried becomes speakable only when the daughter she raised has trained herself to receive it — the female line carrying the inheritance the migration tried to break.

C3 — Mai

Question

How does Pinnock use the character of Mai to explore ideas about cultural inheritance in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Mai

- how Pinnock uses Mai to explore ideas about cultural inheritance.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Mai is Pinnock's mentor-figure and the play's living emblem of Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance — an older Jamaican woman whose Deptford bedsit, weary body, and small client list dramatise how narrowly the practice has had to survive in 1980s Britain, and whose handwritten book of Obeah practice is the inheritance the play argues must be passed on. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 weary practitioner and the messy bedsit, Scene 7 catalyst recognition of "the gift", Scene 8 transfer of the handwritten book.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Obeah / Christian double-register reading (see top of document). Mai's practice is presented as alternative epistemology, not superstition; recovering Mai is recovering the Caribbean cosmology Enid abandoned. Thread into BP1 or BP3.

Technique gloss:

• Naturalism with cultural-inheritance ritual — Mai's Obeah practice held inside naturalistic staging so the inheritance is dignified rather than folklorised.

• Mentor / catalyst figure — Mai recognises Del's gift, sets the deadline, hands the book.

• Recurring object — Mai's handwritten book of Obeah practice as the inheritance in material form.

• Stage direction — Mai's "messy" bedsit (Scene 1) and its cleaned state (Scene 8) as silent characterisation.

• Sound motif — the rooster crowing inside Mai's bedsit (Scene 1) importing rural Jamaica into south London.

• Anagnorisis — Mai's "Del may have the gift of the Obeah" (Scene 7) as recognition that names the inheritance.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Obeah was criminalised in Jamaica under the Obeah Act of 1898, which imposed imprisonment with hard labour on practitioners, and the statute remains on the Jamaican books to this day — meaning that Mai's quiet practice in her Deptford bedsit is the survival of a cultural-religious inheritance the colonial state spent more than a century trying to suppress.

• Building sentence (historical context): Pinnock has written that her own mother and her mother's peers retained the tradition, consulting Obeah practitioners in times of crisis — and the play depicts Mai's practice with the same naturalistic respect it gives the pastor's visit in Scene 2, treating the Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance as religion rather than folklore.

• Thesis: Across her Scene 1 weary practice in the messy Deptford bedsit in which the inheritance looks precariously alive, her Scene 7 recognition of "the gift of the Obeah" in Del in which the inheritance is named for the next generation, and her Scene 8 transfer of the handwritten book of practice in which the inheritance is finally passed on, Pinnock presents Mai as the figure through whom Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance survives the colonial criminalisation, the migration, and the host country's amnesia that combined to threaten it.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 1): weary practitioner and the messy bedsit (7 sentences — about 225 words)

Scene focus: Mai's Deptford bedsit; the bedsit is "messy"; Mai is older, weary, drinking stout; she conducts a clairvoyant reading for Enid; a rooster crows inside the flat.

• Topic sentence: In the play's opening scene, Pinnock establishes Mai as a working practitioner whose room and body register how narrowly the Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance has had to survive in 1980s Britain — the inheritance still present, but only just.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys stage direction inside sound motif — the "messy" opening state of Mai's Deptford bedsit, with Mai older and drinking stout, and a rooster crowing inside the flat as Enid arrives for her reading — combining stage direction (the bedsit's mess as the body of the practitioner, its disorder registering both the weariness of the practitioner and the precariousness of the practice's audience) with sound motif (the rooster crowing inside a south-London bedsit, the rural-Jamaica acoustic geography imported into the room) inside the same opening setting.

• Close analysis: The "messy" bedsit reads first as domestic disorder and then as something larger — a cultural inheritance in decline whose practitioner is exhausted and whose clientele has thinned — while the rooster sound carries the homeland into the room with an uncanniness that registers the cost of the leave-taking even before any dialogue has named it.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the inheritance as something present in the room rather than spoken about — the Jamaican rooster sound, the stout, the worn equipment of the practice all registering the older generation's homeland as still acoustically and physically present.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the precariousness of the survival — an older woman, a thin client list, a messy room, the inheritance held by a single ageing practitioner whose body is failing her.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 1 setting to argue that the Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance is still alive in 1980s Britain but only just — and that the play's central question will be whether it finds its next generation in time.

• Context (AO3): Obeah was criminalised in the British Caribbean colonies from the late eighteenth century — the Jamaican Obeah Act 1898 imposed imprisonment with hard labour — and the practice survived through clandestine transmission across enslavement and migration, reaching 1980s Britain in the condition Pinnock stages in Mai's Deptford bedsit.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 7): catalyst recognition of "the gift" (7 sentences — about 225 words)

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Brod has just told Del the truth about Viv's father; Del kicks the wall; Mai recognises what the kicked wall registers.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed Mai as the weary keeper of the practice, Scene 7 stages her catalytic function — the older practitioner recognising in the second-generation daughter the gift the inheritance has been waiting for.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys anagnorisis inside the catalyst function — Mai's Scene 7 "Del may have the gift of the Obeah" immediately following Del's kicked wall — combining anagnorisis (the moment of recognition that names what the body has just registered, "the gift" a category drawn from the inheritance's own vocabulary rather than from the host country's) with catalyst function (Mai's recognition producing the deadline she sets later in the same act, "you must leave by Wednesday", and the transfer of the book in Scene 8) inside the same exchange.

• Close analysis: The modal "may have" is the play's quietest claim — Pinnock declines to assert the gift as fact, framing it instead as what Mai sees in Del, registering inheritance as something recognised by the older generation rather than declared by the daughter herself.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels Mai's recognition as the play's structural hinge — the moment the inheritance turns from precarious survival in Scene 1 to active transmission in Scene 8.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that inheritance has to be named by an older practitioner before it can be received by a younger one — and that Mai's role in the play is to be the practitioner willing to name it.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Mai's recognition to argue that cultural inheritance does not transfer automatically — it has to be tested, recognised, and handed — and that the gatekeeping is part of the dignity of the practice.

• Context (AO3): Pinnock has framed Obeah in interview as "traces of African spiritual rituals preserved by clandestine practices" — a religious inheritance carrying the memory of an African heritage that enslavement attempted to erase — and Mai's role in the play is the practical embodiment of that preservation.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): transfer of the handwritten book (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Mai passes the "handwritten book" of Obeah practice to Del; Mai withdraws so that Del can read Enid's palm on her own.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 7 had named Del's gift, Scene 8 stages the inheritance's full transfer — Mai handing the handwritten book of practice to her trained successor and withdrawing at the moment the daughter is ready to read on her own.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys recurring object inside structural withdrawal — Mai's Scene 8 transfer of her "handwritten book" of Obeah practice to Del, immediately followed by Mai's quiet withdrawal as Enid arrives as Del's first client — combining recurring object (the book as cultural memory in material form, body-to-body transmission of what enslavement, colonial criminalisation, and migration had tried to erase) with structural withdrawal (Mai's exit from the closing scene as the act of trust that completes the transfer, the mentor stepping aside so the successor can practise) inside the same closing exchange.

• Close analysis: The handwritten quality of the book is part of the argument — the inheritance has reached Del not through print culture but through one practitioner's hand to another, the same hand-to-hand transmission that carried the practice through the colonial criminalisation and across the Atlantic.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels Mai's withdrawal as the act that completes the transfer — the older practitioner trusting the younger to read on her own, the inheritance no longer a thing Mai holds but a thing Del now holds in her place.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the cleaned bedsit as the physical sign of the transfer's success — the room that opened the play "messy" is now ready to be the room of the next practitioner.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be making her clearest argument about cultural inheritance — that it survives by being received rather than preserved, and that Mai's withdrawal is the dignified form of trust the moment of transfer requires.

• Context (AO3): The play premiered on 11 November 1987 at the Liverpool Playhouse Studio and was revived at the Bush Theatre in 2018 with Adjoa Andoh as Mai — the revival opening weeks after the Guardian's reporting on the Windrush deportation scandal, Mai's role registering anew as the keeper of an inheritance the host country was actively threatening.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock constructs Mai as the play's living emblem of Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance — Scene 1 weary practitioner in the messy bedsit, Scene 7 catalyst recognition of "the gift", Scene 8 transfer of the handwritten book — making visible the conditions under which the inheritance has had to survive and the conditions under which it can be passed on.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as precarious survival, Scene 7 named as recognised gift, Scene 8 confirmed as completed transfer — three stages of the same inheritance, the cleaned bedsit at the close measuring the distance the practice has travelled across the eight scenes.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside a 1987 cultural moment in which Black British identity was being formed against a host country that had criminalised the practices the diaspora carried, Pinnock makes Mai the figure through whom the Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance is given its dignity on a British stage.

• Ultimate moral: Mai's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that the inheritance she carries survives not because Pinnock asserts it but because Mai recognises Del, hands her the book, and quietly withdraws — the dignity of the practice contained in the small choreography of the transfer.

C4 — Brod

Question

How does Pinnock use the character of Brod to explore the experience of Caribbean-British men in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Brod

- how Pinnock uses Brod to explore the experience of Caribbean-British men.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Brod is the play's male Windrush figure — Enid's childhood friend from Jamaica, unmarried, lonely, alcoholic, a frequent visitor to her living room — and Pinnock uses him to register what happened to Caribbean-British men whom the host country failed to make space for. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 loose tie and Gullyman's story, Scene 7 trickster disclosure of the truth about Viv's father, Scene 8 quiet withdrawal as the inheritance passes to the daughters.

Technique gloss:

• Mirror character — Brod as Enid's mirror (same generation, opposite outcome) and Del's shadow (the unreceived-inheritance path).

• Trickster of disclosure — character who delivers the play's hardest truth inside disorder.

• Off-stage character — Gullyman (broken by racism), Viv's father (broken by Britain) framing Brod's path.

• Stage direction — Brod's loose tie and old clothes as silent characterisation.

• Recurring object / motif — the rice and peas Brod is denied (Scene 7) standing for the connection he is denied.

• Apposition (close analysis) — Brod's compound naming of the broken father.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The Windrush generation arrived in Britain between 1948 and 1971 carrying British passports under the 1948 British Nationality Act — and yet, by the late 1980s, the legislative chain of the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, the 1971 Immigration Act, and the 1981 British Nationality Act had progressively eroded the right of arrivals like Brod to remain, the same chain that produced the 2018 Windrush deportation scandal.

• Building sentence (historical context): Pinnock places Brod inside Enid's North London living room as the male Windrush figure the play stages on its own terms — alcoholic, unmarried, returning again and again to the household of his childhood friend — and uses the off-stage figures of Gullyman (broken by racism) and Viv's father (broken by Britain) to bracket Brod's path with the outcomes the host country produced for Caribbean-British men.

• Thesis: Across his Scene 2 loose tie and old clothes that register the Windrush generation's exhaustion, his Scene 7 trickster disclosure to Del of the buried truth about Viv's father in which Brod becomes the play's hardest truth-teller, and his Scene 8 quiet withdrawal as the inheritance passes to the daughters in which Brod accepts the role he has been given, Pinnock presents Brod as the figure through whom the male Caribbean-British experience is rendered with seriousness rather than caricature.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 2): loose tie and Gullyman's story (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Enid's living room; the pastor has visited; Brod arrives with his tie hanging loose and his clothes old; he tells Gullyman's story; the Home Office threat to his right to remain surfaces.

• Topic sentence: In the play's second scene, Pinnock establishes Brod as the male Windrush figure whose body and dress register what the host country has cost his generation — the loose tie, the old clothes, and the off-stage Home Office threat all silently characterising a man whose right to belong is being eroded around him.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys stage direction inside the off-stage character function — Brod's Scene 2 loose tie and old clothes set against his retelling of Gullyman's story, "who went mad with grief after he was racially abused" — combining stage direction (the loose tie and old clothes as silent characterisation, the body of the Windrush man registering decades of the host country's friction) with off-stage character function (Gullyman as the off-stage extreme of Brod's path, the friend whose racially-induced breakdown signals what awaits Brod if the trajectory continues) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The loose tie reads first as carelessness and then as exhaustion — a man dressing as he can after decades of two jobs and rented rooms — while Gullyman's off-stage breakdown stages the host country's most extreme outcome for Caribbean-British men without staging the breakdown itself.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels Brod's loneliness as the texture of his entrance — the man who has nowhere else to go visiting the household of the woman he has known since childhood.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Gullyman's off-stage story as the structural mirror of Brod's on-stage one — the play's off-stage spaces telling the audience what the on-stage spaces have been spared by margin.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Brod's Scene 2 entrance to argue that the male Windrush experience is the experience the host country has been most successful at breaking — and that Brod's alcoholism and Gullyman's breakdown are points on the same spectrum.

• Context (AO3): The legislative chain of 1962, 1971 and 1981 had, by Scene 2's 1987 setting, made even Windrush arrivals' right to remain progressively conditional — the same chain whose later iterations produced the 2018 deportation scandal Brod's Scene 2 Home Office threat anticipates.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 7): trickster disclosure about Viv's father (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Brod arrives drunk, complains about being denied rice and peas, and inside the disorder tells Del the buried truth about Viv's father.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed Brod as the male Windrush figure whose body registered the host country's cost, Scene 7 stages his trickster function — the drunken visitor inside whose disorder the play's hardest truth is finally delivered.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys the trickster function inside apposition — Brod's Scene 7 disclosure to Del that Viv's father was "originally a loving and hard-working man who was left broken and 'empty' by the way he was treated in Britain" — combining trickster function (the truth delivered by a drunken visitor whose disorder is the only register inside which the play's hardest disclosure can travel) with apposition (the compound "loving and hard-working" set against "broken and 'empty'", two parallel pairs that name what Britain inherited from him and what Britain returned) inside the same exchange.

• Close analysis: The compound "left broken and 'empty'" is the play's most precise diagnosis of the male Windrush outcome — the past participle "left" registering British racism as the agent and Viv's father as the recipient, while the quoted "empty" (Pinnock's own quotation marks) returns the diagnosis to the man's own vocabulary, registering interiority rather than imposing it.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the disclosure as a release of eighteen years of Del's misreading — the absent father at the centre of her childhood now explained as a man broken by Britain rather than as a man Enid drove away.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the play's hardest truth has been entrusted to its drunkest character, and registers Pinnock's argument that disclosure inside disorder is the only register inside which suppressed family history can finally travel.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Brod's disclosure to argue that the male Windrush experience produced outcomes (Viv's father's violence and absence; Brod's alcoholism; Gullyman's breakdown) that broke the family roles the host country had asked Caribbean-British men to fulfil, and that the daughters of those families have grown up with explanations they have never been given.

• Context (AO3): Caribbean migration to Britain disproportionately damaged the family roles of Caribbean men — the workplace racism, the housing discrimination through the "colour bar" in private rentals, the policing pressure that produced "sus" law arrests — and Pinnock's three on-stage and off-stage male figures (Viv's father, Gullyman, Brod) bracket the play with what the host country did to Caribbean-British masculinity.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): quiet withdrawal as the inheritance passes (7 sentences — about 225 words)

Scene focus: Scene 8; Brod's role in the play has ended with his Scene 7 disclosure; he withdraws so that the inheritance can pass to the daughters.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 7 had stripped Brod to his trickster function, Scene 8 confirms his structural place — the male Windrush figure whose role in the play ends with his disclosure, and whose withdrawal makes room for the female line to carry the inheritance the rest of the way.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural withdrawal paired with Brod's earlier elegiac declarative — Brod's "This place don't want us, this country we work for, this country we build" in the scenes preceding his exit, after which Scene 8's bedsit transfer between Mai and Del unfolds without him — combining structural withdrawal (the male Windrush figure receding from the closing image, the inheritance passing to the female line) with the past-tense register of "work for" and "build" (the labour Brod insists on naming, the country's refusal to acknowledge the building converting his disclosure into the play's reason for handing the inheritance over) inside the same closing structure.

• Close analysis: The absence is the argument — Brod's withdrawal from the closing image is not a failure of the character but a sign that the inheritance has reached the place Brod could not take it, the female line carrying what the male Windrush figure has been broken too far to carry himself.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels Brod's absence from the closing image as quietly poignant — the character who has been the play's most constant visitor receding from the room at the moment the room finally heals.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Brod as Del's shadow — the second-generation outcome the play warns against — and registers that Mai's recognition of Del's "gift" in Scene 7 was the moment that tipped Del away from Brod's path.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Brod's withdrawal to argue that the diasporic inheritance, in 1980s Britain, has had to be carried by the female line because the male Windrush figures the host country produced have, in too many cases, been broken before they could carry it themselves.

• Context (AO3): The off-stage figures of Gullyman (madness), Viv's father (violence and absence), and the on-stage figure of Brod (alcoholism and loneliness) together form the play's structural argument that 1980s Britain produced outcomes for Caribbean-British men which prevented the inheritance from travelling through the male line, and which left the female line to carry it instead.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock constructs Brod as the male Windrush figure whose body, disclosure, and withdrawal together stage what the host country cost Caribbean-British men — Scene 2 loose tie and Gullyman's story, Scene 7 trickster disclosure about Viv's father, Scene 8 quiet withdrawal as the inheritance passes to the daughters.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as the body of the broken Windrush man, Scene 7 named through the disclosure of the broken father, Scene 8 confirmed by the withdrawal that lets the female line carry the inheritance — three stages of the same diagnosis, the male path the play warns against and the female path the play hopes for.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock honours Brod by giving him the play's hardest truth to deliver and then letting him withdraw — refusing both the saint and the cautionary tale, treating Brod as a man whose body has carried what the host country handed him and whose disclosure has earned him the right to recede.

• Ultimate moral: Brod's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that he is the play's quietest indictment — the male Windrush figure whose breakage is not addressed by any character and whose disclosure makes the daughters' coming-of-age possible.

C5 — The female line: Enid, Mai and Del across three generations

Question

How does Pinnock present the three generations of women in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Enid, Mai and Del at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses the three generations of women to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Pinnock builds the play around three on-stage Caribbean-British women — Mai (older mentor and chosen mother), Enid (Windrush biological mother), and Del (British-born second-generation daughter) — whose three positions form a female line through which the diasporic inheritance has had to travel because the male line has been broken by the host country. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 the three women separated by their separate responses to the practice, Scene 6 Enid's consultation as the older line consulting the older line, Scene 8 the three positions converging in the cleaned bedsit.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Caribbean-British double-consciousness reading. The female line carries what the male line cannot; the three generations across three rooms answer the silence the host country imposed. Thread into BP3 or the conclusion.

Technique gloss:

• Female-line structure — three Caribbean-British women across three generations as the play's structural device.

• Foil-pairing — Mai and Enid (mentor vs biological), Enid and Del (mother vs daughter), Mai and Del (mentor vs successor).

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — Mai's practice as the room inside which the three generations finally meet.

C6 — Enid and Brod as the Windrush generation

Question

Frame. Enid and Brod are Pinnock's two on-stage Windrush figures — the woman who carried the household and the man the host country broke — and Pinnock pairs them as childhood friends from Jamaica whose 1980s London lives register the doubled outcome the migration produced for the female and male lines. INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The MV Empire Windrush docked at Tilbury on 22 June 1948 carrying 492 passengers from the Caribbean — and across the next twenty-three years the Windrush generation arrived in Britain carrying British passports under the 1948 British Nationality Act, leaving behind homelands and parents many would never return to see again; Pinnock's Enid and Brod are two of those arrivals staged inside the same North London living room four decades on.

• Building sentence (historical context): Pinnock places Enid and Brod as childhood friends from Jamaica whose 1980s London lives register the doubled outcome the migration produced — Enid holding the household through two jobs and an eighteen-year silence, Brod returning again and again to the household of his friend because the host country has left him nowhere else to go.

Scene focus: Enid's North London living room; Brod visits with his loose tie and old clothes; Gullyman's off-stage story; the Home Office threat to Brod surfaces.

• Topic sentence: In the play's second scene, Pinnock places Enid and Brod inside the same Windrush living room and registers their two outcomes through their two bodies — Enid's weary mid-shift presence and Brod's loose tie and old clothes naming, before any dialogue, the doubled cost the migration has extracted.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Enid and Brod as the play's two on-stage Windrush figures, and registers that the household the play has chosen is the household where the female line has held while the male line has been broken around it.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 2 pairing to argue that the Windrush experience produced two paths through 1980s Britain — the woman who held and the man the host country broke — and that both paths are inside the same household because the friendship is the diasporic version of belonging the host country has left available.

• Context (AO3): The Windrush generation arrived under the 1948 British Nationality Act with full British citizenship and was recruited to fill post-war labour shortages — particularly in the NHS founded the same year — yet the legislative chain of 1962, 1971 and 1981 progressively eroded the rights the 1948 Act granted, the legislative chain producing exactly the doubled outcome Pinnock stages in Scene 2.

Scene focus: Scene 4 Enid's grieving asyndetic list and the saved university money for Viv; Scene 7 Brod's drunken disclosure about Viv's father.

Callaloo" paired with Brod's Scene 7 apposition "originally a loving and hard-working man who was left broken and 'empty' by the way he was treated in Britain" — combining parallel disclosure (the two Windrush characters each in turn naming the cost of the migration, one through grieving syntax, one through drunken apposition) with two registers (the female outcome delivered through unfinished mourning, the male outcome delivered through trickster disorder) inside the same diasporic household.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the Windrush generation has not been one experience but two paths through the same legislative chain — and that Pinnock's pairing of Enid and Brod is the structural staging of that doubled outcome.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as the doubled Windrush cost, Scenes 4 + 7 named as the parallel female and male outcomes, Scene 8 confirmed as the female line carrying what the male line could not — three stages of the same diagnosis, the Windrush generation's two paths inside one household.
