• Broken speech (Scene 8) — Enid's confession.

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — Del's palm-reading as the room inside which the relationship finally meets.

• Doubled coming-of-age — both Enid's truth-telling arc and Del's reception arc must move.

• Stage business — Del's stolen charm, kicked wall, and cleaning of Mai's bedsit as the relationship's wordless beats.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Pinnock has written that she drew on the North London household founded by her own Jamaican parents — and the Enid–Del relationship is her play's central mother-daughter axis, an eighteen-year diasporic relationship dramatised across eight scenes in which the Windrush mother and the British-born daughter have to invent a relationship the host country has refused to make room for.

• Building sentence (historical context): The relationship sits inside the legislative chain (1962 CIA / 1971 IA / 1981 BNA) that progressively eroded the Windrush generation's right to remain and forced the second-generation daughters to come of age inside households whose original homeland was no longer geographically reachable, and the play's structure traces the relationship's three stages — friction, cost, repair.

• Thesis: Across Scene 1 in which Enid's patois rebuke and Del's silent stolen charm install the relationship as a conflict of register, Scene 4 in which Enid's grieving asyndetic list and the saved university money reveal the relationship as the diasporic redirection of inheritance, and Scene 8 in which the broken-speech confession and palm-reading stage the relationship's possible repair through the trained daughter's reception, Pinnock dramatises the Enid–Del relationship as the structural axis through which the diasporic mother-daughter healing has to travel.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 1): patois discipline meets stolen charm (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Enid's patois confrontation with Del; Del steals a charm from Mai's table; the relationship registered in two registers at once.

• Topic sentence: In the play's opening, Pinnock installs the Enid–Del relationship as the friction of two registers — the Windrush mother's homeland-grammar set against the British-born daughter's silent refusal, the eighteen years of household life compressed into a single confrontation.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys patois-versus-London-speech contrast inside stage business — Enid's Scene 1 "You tink dis is Jamaica? You tink you can jus' walk outta mi 'ouse" answered by Del's silent stealing of a charm from Mai's table — combining patois-versus-London-speech contrast (the homeland register set against the host-country register, the relationship's two languages registered in the same exchange) with stage business (Del's wordless theft as the second generation's answer to the first generation's spoken authority) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The simultaneity of patois and silent theft is the relationship's first staged paradox — Enid's authority is loud and registered in a register Del does not share, Del's refusal is silent and registered in a body Enid has not learnt to read, the relationship operating in two registers neither side has been able to translate.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the relationship's friction in the sounds of the conversation before they hear it in the argument's content — the daughter who cannot answer in patois and the mother who cannot read the silent theft.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the relationship in this household has been operating without a shared register — and registers that the relationship's eventual repair will depend on inventing one.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Scene 1 to argue that the diasporic mother-daughter relationship cannot be repaired inside the household's existing registers — the Windrush patois and the south-east London speech cannot meet without a third space, and the play's work will be finding that space.

• Context (AO3): The Windrush generation arrived in Britain between 1948 and 1971 carrying patois as the speech of their childhoods, and the British-born second generation grew up in households where every mother-daughter exchange staged the diasporic question in two registers at once.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 4): the saved university money and the off-stage homeland-mother (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: Enid grieving on the sofa with Viv after the telephone has carried news of her own mother's death in Jamaica; the disclosure of the saved university money for Viv; the relationship between Enid and Del registered in absence rather than presence.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed the relationship as a conflict of register, Scene 4 reveals what the relationship has been carrying — Enid's silence about her own mother in Jamaica, the saved university money that redirects the homeland inheritance into a British future Enid herself will not share.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys absence inside disclosure — Del's structural absence from Scene 4 (she is not present for Enid's grieving) set against Enid's asyndetic list "Old time woman could cook. Roast breadfruit. Callaloo" and her disclosure of the saved university money for Viv — combining absence (the relationship registered as Del's not being in the room when her mother is grieving) with disclosure (Enid's grief, the food list, the saved money all delivered to Viv rather than to Del) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: Del's absence from Scene 4 is the play's quietest indictment of the relationship's current state — the elder daughter is not in the room when the Windrush mother is grieving the homeland-mother, the relationship has reached the point at which the cost is being absorbed in silence.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the relationship as something that has been carrying more than either character can yet articulate — the saved money, the dying off-stage grandmother, the absent Del all registering the relationship's eighteen-year accumulation.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the relationship has been redirected — from the homeland-mother Enid could not return to, to the saved university money that becomes the only inheritance she can give — and registers that Del's absence is itself part of the cost.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Scene 4 to argue that the diasporic mother-daughter relationship has been carrying more than the household has been able to articulate — and that the relationship's repair will require both halves to enter the same room with what has been redirected through them.

• Context (AO3): Most Caribbean migrants arrived on British-subject passports the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act retrospectively voided — that documentary erasure became the 2018 Windrush scandal — and by the 1980s the legislative chain had made return travel for the Windrush generation legally and economically harder than for the original arrivals; Enid's absence from her own mother's funeral is the legislative chain's pressure on the Enid–Del relationship.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): broken-speech confession and palm-reading (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Del has been trained; Enid arrives as Del's first client; the eighteen-year silence breaks; Del reads her mother's palm.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 4 had revealed the relationship's accumulated cost, Scene 8 stages its possible repair — the trained daughter finally able to make the room inside which the Windrush mother can speak, the relationship arriving in a third register the play has had to invent across the eight scenes.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys broken speech inside cultural-inheritance ritual — Enid's halting "You think life kind to me?" delivered in Mai's cleaned bedsit as Del begins the palm-reading — combining broken speech (the interrogative incomplete in the second-person, the eighteen-year silence breaking inside one direct question that has waited the whole play to be asked) with the cultural-inheritance ritual (the palm-reading as the third register the relationship has had to invent, the body-to-body practice the chosen mother trained the daughter in) inside the same closing exchange.

• Close analysis: The third register is the play's argument — the relationship could not be repaired in patois (Enid's homeland-grammar Del did not share) or in south-east London speech (Del's host-country-grammar Enid could not enter); only in the cultural-inheritance ritual the older generation kept can the relationship finally arrive at the same room.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the relationship's repair as a daughter's hand on a mother's palm — the inherited practice as the medium of touch, the eighteen-year silence breaking inside the room the chosen mother kept for the moment the daughter would be ready.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the diasporic mother-daughter relationship requires a third space — neither homeland nor host country, but the inherited practice the older generation has been carrying — and registers Scene 8 as that third space staged in one quiet image.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 8 palm-reading to argue that the diasporic mother-daughter relationship cannot be repaired inside the household's existing registers — and that the trained daughter has to make a third room, the chosen-mother's room, inside which the silenced truths can finally be received.

• Context (AO3): Stuart Hall's Cultural Identity and Diaspora (1990) names diasporic identity as "a matter of 'becoming' as well as of 'being'" — and the Enid–Del Scene 8 palm-reading is exactly that becoming, the diasporic mother-daughter relationship registered as a practice the daughter has begun rather than a state either side has reached.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises the Enid–Del relationship through three stages — Scene 1 patois discipline meeting stolen charm, Scene 4 the saved university money and the off-stage homeland-mother revealing the relationship's redirected inheritance, Scene 8 broken-speech confession and palm-reading staging the relationship's repair inside the third register the play has had to invent.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as the relationship's conflict of register, Scene 4 named as the relationship's accumulated cost, Scene 8 began to repair through the trained daughter's reception of the inherited practice — three stages of the same diasporic mother-daughter axis, the relationship arriving at the same room only when the older generation breaks its silence and the younger generation has cleaned the space.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1987 inside the Royal Court Young Writers' Group and the formation of Black British identity, Pinnock honours the diasporic mother-daughter relationship as the structural axis through which the female-line healing has to travel.

• Ultimate moral: The Enid–Del relationship in Leave Taking arrives at the same room only inside the third register the older generation kept for it — and the closing palm-reading is the relationship's repair staged as one daughter's hand on one mother's palm.

C9 — Del and Viv as sisters

Question

How does Pinnock present the relationship between Del and Viv in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Del and Viv at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses the relationship between Del and Viv to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Del and Viv are Pinnock's two routes into the diasporic inheritance — the rebellious elder daughter who refuses then receives the body-to-body practice, and the studious younger sister whose academic withdrawal converts in Scene 8 into the formal-educational claiming of Black Studies. Pinnock holds the two paths against each other to argue that the diasporic second generation has had to invent more than one route home, and that the female line's two responses converge in the play's closing image. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 contrasting first responses to Mai's Obeah practice, Scene 5 protective eviction at the missed exam, Scene 8 convergence of the two routes through the handwritten book and Black Studies.

Technique gloss:

• Foil character / sibling pair — Del and Viv as the two-route response of the second generation to the same household pressure.

• Stage business — Del's stolen charm, Viv's silent revision, Del's protective push of Viv out of the bedsit.

• Off-stage event — Viv's missed English Literature exam.

• Doubled coming-of-age — Del's body-to-body reception arc and Viv's academic-formal arc as parallel trajectories.

• Structural convergence — Scene 8 staging the two routes meeting at the close.

• Code-switching (analytical category) — both daughters negotiating between patois household and standard-English school.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The Caribbean Education in Britain Working Party reported in 1971 that Caribbean-British students were being placed in "educationally subnormal" schools at four times the rate of their white peers — the same misdiagnosis that the school in Leave Taking applies to Del and that produces the contrasting paths the two daughters take across the play's eight scenes.

• Building sentence (historical context): Pinnock has written that she drew on her own teenage experience and on the experiences of the Caribbean-British young women around her — and the two daughters she places inside Enid's North London household are constructed as the two-route response the second generation invented to the same household pressure, the rebellious elder sister whose schooling failed her and the studious younger sister whose schooling examined her on a canon Pinnock has herself described as one in which she "could not find a trace of" her experience.

• Thesis: Across Scene 1 in which the two daughters' contrasting first responses to Mai's Obeah practice install Del as the rebel and Viv as the careful interpreter, Scene 5 in which Del's protective push of Viv out of the bedsit at the missed English Literature exam stages the two routes diverging under household pressure, and Scene 8 in which Viv's announcement of Black Studies at university converges with Del's reception of Mai's handwritten book on the same horizon, Pinnock presents the Del-Viv relationship as the female line's structural argument that the diasporic inheritance can be claimed in more than one register at once.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 1): contrasting first responses to Mai's practice (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Mai's Deptford bedsit; Mai conducts a clairvoyant reading for Enid; Del watches with hostile resistance and steals a charm; Viv positions herself differently, careful and observing rather than openly hostile.

• Topic sentence: In the play's opening, Pinnock establishes Del and Viv as the household's two contrasting routes through the same diasporic pressure — the elder sister responding to Mai's practice through hostile theft, the younger sister responding through careful watchfulness, the two routes installed before any household crisis has yet forced them to diverge.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys foil-pairing inside Del's bitter declarative — Del's "I'm not even allowed to be black if it suits you" spoken in Mai's bedsit as she pockets the charm, Viv's silent watchfulness behind her — combining foil-pairing (Del's hostile-rebel posture against Viv's careful-observer posture, the two daughters of the same household responding to identical pressure with opposite first instincts) with the deictic grammar of "allowed" (the modal converting maternal authority into permission-economics, Del's refusal arriving as her speech rather than her body though her body is simultaneously enacting the theft) inside the same opening setting.

• Close analysis: The simultaneity of Del's verbal dismissal and physical theft installs her as the daughter whose body acknowledges what her speech denies, while Viv's careful watchfulness installs her as the daughter who has chosen a different strategy — assimilation through attention rather than refusal through theft, the two routes already structurally distinct in the play's first scene.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the sibling contrast as the friction inside which the second generation has been raised — two daughters of the same household responding to the same pressure in opposite registers, the elder through hostile rebellion, the younger through careful preparation.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the household has produced two routes rather than one — and registers that the play's argument will depend on whether either route can carry the inheritance the older generation has been holding.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 1 contrast to argue that the diasporic second generation has had to invent more than one strategy in response to the host country's pressure — and that the play will need both daughters' routes to make its closing argument about the inheritance.

• Context (AO3): In 1980s Britain, Caribbean-British young women responded to the same school-system pressure in distinct ways — some through assimilation through education, some through alternative routes the formal system did not recognise — and Pinnock's two daughters embody the two-route response of an entire second-generation cohort.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 5): protective eviction at the missed English Literature exam (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Del visibly pregnant has moved in with Mai; Viv has come to find her sister; the off-stage English Literature exam is being sat without her; Del pushes Viv out of the room.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed the two routes as the household's contrasting first responses, Scene 5 stages their first divergence under crisis — Del refusing to let her sister's route be ruined by association with hers, the elder sister's protective eviction registering the cost the household pressure has begun to extract.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys protective stage business inside the off-stage event — Del's "You should not ruin your chances" eviction of Viv from Mai's bedsit, framed by the off-stage English Literature exam being sat without her — combining protective stage business (Del physically pushing Viv out of the room, the elder sister's care delivered as refusal of contact rather than as embrace) with off-stage event (the exam taking place in a room the play declines to stage, the institution kept off-stage like every other antagonistic British space) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The modal "should not" registers Del's protective imperative as care delivered through structure rather than through tenderness — the elder sister, by her own account ruined by the household pressure, refusing to let the younger sister's route be contaminated, the female line's responsibility for the next generation taking the form of strategic separation.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels Del's eviction as the older sister's protective version of love — refusing to let her sister's route be ruined by association with hers, the divergence between the two daughters' paths now structural and self-imposed rather than circumstantial.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the household pressure has already converted the two routes from contrasting first instincts into divergent paths — Del's pregnancy and Viv's missed exam as the structural cost the female line has been forced to absorb, the second generation's two responses now visibly separating.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Del's protective eviction to argue that the diasporic sibling relationship in 1980s Britain has had to operate inside a household pressure that produced costly divergence — that the elder sister's care for the younger has had to take the form of strategic separation rather than shared shelter, and that the cost is the form the household pressure has imposed.

• Context (AO3): The English Literature canon Caribbean-British students were examined on in 1980s Britain produced the Black British literary movement (Caryl Phillips's The Final Passage in 1985, the Black British women writers Pinnock joined) — the absence of "a trace of" the second generation's experience in the official canon being the structural pressure that produced the divergent responses Del's protective eviction stages.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): convergence of the two routes (7 sentences — about 225 words)

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Del receives the handwritten book; Viv announces she will study Black Studies at university; the two daughters' contrasting routes meet on the same horizon.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 5 had stripped the two routes to divergent paths under crisis, Scene 8 stages their convergence on the same horizon — Viv's academic claiming of Black Studies meeting Del's body-to-body reception of Mai's handwritten book, the two routes the female line invented arriving at the same inheritance in two complementary registers.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural convergence inside Viv's declarative announcement — Viv's "I'm going to study Black Studies" set against Del's reception of Mai's handwritten book in the same Scene 8 cleaned bedsit — combining structural convergence (the academic and the body-to-body forms of the inheritance arriving as complementary rather than competing) with the modal of intention "going to study" (Viv's first-person future commitment converting her assimilation route into a reclamation route, the same noun "Black" that Del's mother-tongue has been speaking now entering the academic register as a discipline) inside the same closing exchange.

• Close analysis: The naming of "Black Studies" in Viv's announcement converts her assimilation route into reclamation of the inheritance the canon had refused, while the handwritten book registers the body-to-body alternative — the same cultural inheritance arriving in the academy through Viv's choice and in the bedsit through Mai's transfer, the female line's two routes the second generation invented arriving at the same destination by different forms.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the convergence as the play's quietly hopeful answer to the household pressure that drove the two routes apart in Scene 5 — the divergent paths the female line was forced to invent meeting on the same horizon, the inheritance arriving in both registers because the second generation needed both routes to claim it.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the doubled claiming as the play's structural argument that the diasporic inheritance can be received in more than one form — and registers that the sibling relationship the play opened with as contrasting routes has resolved as complementary halves of the same female-line answer.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be making her clearest argument about the sibling relationship here — that the diasporic second generation has had to invent two routes home rather than one, and that the female line's two responses converge as the academic and the body-to-body forms of the same inheritance the host country had tried to break.

• Context (AO3): The first British academic Centre for the Study of Race and Ethnicity emerged in the 1980s and the Black British literary and cultural studies movement Pinnock joined gave Caribbean-British students the formal-educational route Viv announces, while the body-to-body cultural inheritance Mai transfers to Del was the alternative the formal canon had refused to recognise — Pinnock's argument being that both routes were needed for the female line to claim the inheritance the migration tried to break.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises the Del-Viv sibling relationship as the female line's two-route response to the same household pressure — Scene 1 contrasting first responses to Mai's practice, Scene 5 protective eviction at the missed exam, Scene 8 convergence of the two routes through the handwritten book and Black Studies.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as the two daughters' contrasting first instincts, Scene 5 stripped to divergent paths under household crisis, Scene 8 confirmed as complementary halves of the same female-line claiming — three stages of the same sibling argument, the second generation needing both routes to receive the inheritance the migration tried to erase.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside the 1987 cultural moment that gave the diasporic second generation the formal-educational route Viv chooses, Pinnock honours both daughters equally — refusing to elevate either route over the other and arguing that the female line's claiming of the inheritance has had to operate in more than one register.

• Ultimate moral: The Del-Viv sibling relationship in Leave Taking is the play's structural argument that the diasporic inheritance can be received in more than one form — and the closing image, with Del's hand on Enid's palm and Viv's Black Studies announcement framing it, is the female line's two routes meeting on the same horizon at last.

C10 — Viv

Question

How does Pinnock present Viv in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Viv at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses Viv to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Viv is Del's foil and Pinnock's second route to claiming the inheritance — the studious sixteen-year-old GCSE student whose assimilation through education looks at first like the opposite of Del's Obeah training, but whose Scene 8 decision to study Black Studies at university converges with Del's path on the same horizon. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 mediating audience-surrogate, Scene 5 missed exam and divergence, Scene 8 university and convergence.

Technique gloss:

• Foil character — character whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Viv to Del).

• Audience surrogate — character who asks the questions the audience is asking (Viv's "And what if I fail my exams?").

• Off-stage event — event that shapes the action without being staged (the missed English Literature exam).

• Code-switching (analytical category) — Viv negotiates between patois household and standard-English school.

• Structural convergence — two character arcs that look divergent but meet at the close (Viv's Black Studies, Del's Obeah).

• Stage direction — Viv's revision in the cramped living room as silent characterisation.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Pinnock herself sat A-levels in the early 1980s in a North London household founded by Jamaican parents — and like Viv she went on to university, eventually completing a PhD on Black British playwriting at Goldsmiths — making Viv the character closest to the author's own teenage self in the play's three-generation household.

• Building sentence (historical context): Viv's GCSE-equivalent English Literature exam sits inside the literary canon Pinnock has herself described in interview as one in which she "could not find a trace of" her experience, and the missed exam in Scene 5 is the play's most pointed structural indictment of an education system that treated Caribbean-British students as if their inheritance did not count.

• Thesis: Across her Scene 2 mediating role between the older generation's patois and the younger generation's south-east London speech in which Viv functions as Pinnock's audience-surrogate, her Scene 5 missed English Literature exam in which Viv's path appears to diverge from Del's, and her Scene 8 announcement that she has chosen to study Black Studies at university in which the two daughters' routes converge, Pinnock presents Viv as the second route to claiming the diasporic inheritance — formal-educational where Del's is body-to-body.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 2): mediating audience-surrogate (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Enid's living room; the pastor visits; Brod arrives and tells Gullyman's story; Viv mediates between the older generation's patois and her own south-east London speech.

• Topic sentence: In the play's opening movement, Pinnock establishes Viv as the mediating second-generation daughter — careful, studious, the family's hope for upward mobility — and uses her as the audience-surrogate through whom the household becomes legible.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys audience-surrogate questioning inside foil-contrast — Viv's careful mediating presence in Scene 2, audible in lines such as "And what if I fail my exams? What'll she do then?" (Scene 2) — combining audience-surrogate (Viv asking on the audience's behalf the question they want to ask of the play, what is the cost if assimilation does not work?) with foil-contrast (Viv's careful register set against Del's sullen sarcasm, the two daughters of the same household responding to identical pressures with opposite strategies) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The conditional "if I fail" registers a sixteen-year-old who has internalised the host country's verdict in advance — assimilation through education is presented as the gamble on which the family's future has been staked, and Viv has already started counting the cost.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears Viv as the household's careful interpreter — the daughter who reads, who studies, who carries the older generation's hopes into the school the host country has provided.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Viv as the formal-educational route the family has bet on — and registers the precarity of betting on it inside an exam system whose canon will not contain her.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Viv to ask the audience the question the audience needs to ask of the play — what if assimilation through education does not work, and what is the household supposed to fall back on if it fails?

• Context (AO3): Education was the principal route of upward mobility offered to Caribbean-British young people in 1980s Britain, and the gap between the canon they were examined on and the inheritance they came from was, for Pinnock and her generation, the central friction of teenage life inside a Windrush household.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 5): the missed English Literature exam (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Del visibly pregnant; Viv has come to find her sister; the off-stage English Literature exam is being sat without her; Del pushes Viv out of the room.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed Viv as the family's careful interpreter, Scene 5 stages the cost of her route — the missed English Literature exam dramatising the system's refusal to make space for the inheritance Viv carries, even as the daughter herself remains willing to do everything the system asks.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys the off-stage event inside Del's eviction — Viv's missed English Literature exam (Scene 5) framed by Del's "You should not ruin your chances" refusal to let Viv stay in the bedsit — combining off-stage event (the exam taking place in a room the play declines to stage, the institution kept off-stage like every other antagonistic British space) with Del's protective eviction (the older sister refusing to let the younger sacrifice the route the family has bet on, even at the cost of the bedsit refuge) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The exam's off-stage status is part of the argument — Pinnock declines to stage the institution that examines Caribbean-British students on a canon in which they cannot find themselves, and the absence is itself the indictment, the same withholding that keeps Enid's hospital cleaning, Del's racist workplace, and Brod's Home Office threat off-stage.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the cost of the missed exam not as Viv's individual failure but as the system's accumulated pressure on a sixteen-year-old whose family has bet everything on this single afternoon.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Del's eviction as the older sister's protective version of love — refusing to let her sister's route be ruined by association with hers, the divergence between the two daughters' paths now structural.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the missed exam to argue that the assimilation route is far more precarious than the family's investment in it admits — and that the precarity is produced not by the daughter's failures but by an institution that has never made room for the inheritance she carries.

• Context (AO3): In 1980s Britain, Caribbean-British school students were disproportionately under-served by the school system — the gap between the GCSE English Literature canon and the literature their families had brought with them produced the Black British literary movement (Caryl Phillips's The Final Passage, 1985) Pinnock herself was part of.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): Black Studies at university and convergence (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Scene 8; Viv announces she has chosen to study Black Studies at university; the route that looked divergent from Del's Obeah training meets it on the same horizon.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 5 had revealed the cost of Viv's route, Scene 8 confirms its eventual destination — the studious daughter who had been examined on a literature canon in which she could find no trace of herself now chooses to study, at university, the inheritance the family has been carrying all along.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural convergence inside Viv's declarative announcement — Viv's "I'm going to study Black Studies" paired with Del's silent reception of Mai's handwritten book in the same Scene 8 cleaned bedsit — combining structural convergence (formal-educational claiming on Viv's side, body-to-body claiming on Del's) with cyclical structure (the unchanged room measuring the changed transmission, the inheritance arriving in both daughters by the closing scene in the two registers each invented for herself) inside the same closing image.

• Close analysis: The naming of "Black Studies" in Viv's announcement is the formal-educational version of the cultural inheritance Mai carries in handwritten form — the same cultural memory entering the daughter's life through the academy in Viv's case and through the bedsit in Del's, the play arguing that two routes are open and that both lead home.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the convergence as the play's most quietly hopeful image — the assimilation route and the inheritance route, set against each other across the eight scenes, finally meeting in the same closing minutes.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the doubled claiming as the play's structural answer to the controlling concept — the diasporic second generation can claim the inheritance the host country taught them to refuse, and it can do so in more than one register.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Viv's Black Studies decision to argue that the formal-educational route can be reclaimed for the inheritance rather than weaponised against it — and that the academy, when it makes room for the diasporic canon, can be a route to claiming what assimilation tried to erase.

• Context (AO3): The first British academic Centre for the Study of Race and Ethnicity emerged in the 1980s, and the Black British literary and cultural studies movement Pinnock herself joined gave Caribbean-British students, by the late 1980s, the formal-educational route Viv announces she has chosen.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock constructs Viv as Del's foil and the play's second route to claiming the diasporic inheritance — Scene 2 mediating audience-surrogate, Scene 5 cost of the assimilation route through the missed exam, Scene 8 convergence of the two daughters' paths in Black Studies and Obeah training.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as the family's bet on assimilation through education, Scene 5 exposed as a route the institution had never made room for, Scene 8 reclaimed as a route the daughter could refuse on her own terms — the inheritance entering Viv's life through the academy as it enters Del's through the bedsit.

• Author's purpose: Writing closer to Viv than to any other character in the play, Pinnock honours the studious second-generation daughter whose route the canon had not made room for, and whose eventual claiming of Black Studies is the formal-educational version of the inheritance she was always already carrying.

• Ultimate moral: Viv's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that the play needs both daughters' routes — academy and bedsit, Black Studies and Obeah, the formal and the body-to-body — to make its argument that the diasporic inheritance can be received in more than one register.

T1 — Identity, Britishness and Caribbean inheritance

Question

How does Pinnock present ideas about identity in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Pinnock presents ideas about identity.

Frame. INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Pinnock has framed Obeah in interview as "traces of African spiritual rituals preserved by clandestine practices" — a cultural inheritance carrying the memory of an African heritage that enslavement attempted to erase, criminalised in Jamaica under the Obeah Act of 1898 and surviving through clandestine transmission across the diaspora to reach 1980s Britain in the form Pinnock stages in Mai's Deptford bedsit.

• Building sentence (historical context): Cultural inheritance in Leave Taking operates across three vehicles — patois (the language the Windrush generation brought), Obeah practice (the spiritual tradition the older generation kept), and the handwritten book (the material form the inheritance travels in) — and Pinnock holds all three inside the naturalistic staging that gives the inheritance its dignity rather than reducing it to folklore.

• Thesis: Across Scene 1 in which the messy bedsit, the rooster's crow and Mai's weary practice register the cultural inheritance as precariously alive, Scene 4 in which Enid's asyndetic list of Jamaican dishes and the saved university money stage the inheritance as redirected through the diaspora's available forms, and Scene 8 in which the handwritten book and the closing palm-reading complete the body-to-body transfer of the inheritance to the second generation, Pinnock dramatises cultural inheritance as a system of knowledge the diasporic family has had to keep clandestinely against the host country's amnesia.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 1): inheritance precariously alive (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Mai's Deptford bedsit; the bedsit is "messy"; a rooster crows inside the flat; Mai is older, weary, drinking stout; she conducts a clairvoyant reading for Enid; Del watches with hostile resistance.

• Close analysis: The naturalistic register is the argument — Pinnock declines to lift the inheritance into folklore or exotica, treating Mai's practice as serious cultural knowledge — while the rooster sound registers the homeland's continuing presence as something the leave-taking could not fully erase.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the cultural inheritance as something present in the room rather than spoken about — the Jamaican rooster sound, the stout, the worn equipment of the practice all registering the inheritance as a daily working life.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the precariousness — an older woman, a thin client list, a messy room, the inheritance held by a single ageing practitioner — and registers that the play's question will be whether the inheritance finds its next carrier in time.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 1 setting to argue that the cultural inheritance the diaspora has been carrying is alive but narrowly so in 1980s Britain — and that the play's central question will be its transmission to the next generation.

• Context (AO3): Obeah was criminalised under the Jamaican Obeah Act 1898 — imprisonment with hard labour for practitioners — and the practice survived through clandestine transmission across enslavement and migration, reaching 1980s Britain in the condition Pinnock stages in Mai's bedsit.

Scene focus: Enid grieving on the sofa with Viv after the telephone has carried news of her mother's death in Jamaica; the disclosure of the saved university money for Viv; the homeland assembled in fragments of food.

Callaloo" framed by her disclosure that she has saved a large sum of money for Viv's university — combining asyndeton (the three Jamaican dishes set down without conjunctions, the food inheritance reduced to fragments) with the saved-money motif (the inheritance the host country accepts, redirected from the homeland to a British university place) inside the same nostalgic exchange.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the closing palm-reading as the inheritance arriving in its earned form — the cultural knowledge transferred body-to-body, the room cleaned, the practitioner trained, the first client a Windrush mother whose silenced truths the practice can finally hold.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the inheritance has survived not by being preserved but by being received — and that Pinnock's argument is for transmission as the inheritance's only available form of life.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be making her clearest argument about cultural inheritance here — that it survives through body-to-body transmission rather than through institutional protection, and that the diaspora's task has been keeping the practice alive long enough for the next generation to receive it.

• Context (AO3): Paul Gilroy's The Black Atlantic (1993) describes the inheritance of African religious practice carried through the Middle Passage and the diaspora as the structural form of Black-Atlantic identity — and Mai's handwritten book is exactly that inheritance staged in 1987 in dramatic miniature.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises cultural inheritance through three stages — Scene 1 inheritance precariously alive in the messy bedsit, Scene 4 inheritance redirected through food fragments and saved money, Scene 8 inheritance transferred body-to-body through the handwritten book and palm-reading.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as the precarious daily life of the inheritance, Scene 4 named as the redirection the leave-taking imposed, Scene 8 confirmed as completed transmission — three stages of the same inheritance, the diaspora's cultural knowledge kept alive through body-to-body transmission against the host country's amnesia.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside a 1987 cultural moment that had inherited the colonial criminalisation of Afro-Caribbean spiritual practice, Pinnock makes the inheritance the play's central material — refusing both the exoticising lens of colonial fascination and the dismissive lens of rationalist contempt.

• Ultimate moral: Cultural inheritance in Leave Taking survives not because the diaspora protects it but because the diaspora transmits it — and the closing palm-reading is one practitioner's body-to-body transfer staged as the form the inheritance has had to take to reach the next generation.

T5 — Coming-of-age and change

Question

How does Pinnock present ideas about coming of age in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents change in young characters

- how Pinnock uses coming-of-age to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Coming-of-age in Leave Taking is the doubled arc of two second-generation daughters — Del's body-to-body reception arc and Viv's formal-educational arc — and the play's argument that the diasporic second generation has had to invent its own coming-of-age inside a host country that refused to make room for it. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 the two daughters' initial registers, Scene 5 the divergence under household pressure, Scene 8 the converged coming-of-age in two complementary forms.

Technique gloss:

• Coming-of-age / Bildungsroman — the protagonist moves from innocence through experience to maturity; here doubled across two daughters.

• Foil-pairing — Del's body-to-body arc set against Viv's formal-educational arc.

• Stage business — Del's stolen charm, kicked wall, cleaning of the bedsit as the wordless beats of her arc.

• Off-stage event — Viv's missed exam as the institutional refusal her arc has to absorb.

• Anagnorisis — Mai's recognition of Del's "gift" (Scene 7) as the moment the second generation's coming-of-age becomes possible.

• Structural convergence — Scene 8's closing image as the two daughters' coming-of-age completed in two registers.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The diasporic Bildungsroman — Caryl Phillips's The Final Passage (1985), Joan Riley's The Unbelonging (1985), Pinnock's own Leave Taking (1987) — emerged as a recognisable Black British literary form in the 1980s, and Pinnock's two daughters across the play's eight scenes are the dramatic embodiment of that form.

• Building sentence (historical context): Coming-of-age for the diasporic second generation in 1980s Britain had to be invented against an education system that placed Caribbean-British students in "educationally subnormal" schools at four times the rate of white peers (Caribbean Education in Britain Working Party, 1971), a workplace that treated them as if they did not "speak English", and a literary canon Pinnock has described as one in which she "could not find a trace of" her own experience.

• Thesis: Across Scene 1 in which Del's hostile theft and Viv's careful watchfulness install the two daughters' contrasting registers, Scene 5 in which Del's pregnancy and Viv's missed English Literature exam stage the divergence under household pressure, and Scene 8 in which the handwritten book and the Black Studies announcement converge the two daughters' arcs on the same horizon, Pinnock dramatises coming-of-age as the doubled arc through which the diasporic second generation has had to invent the maturity the host country had not made room for.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 1): two daughters, two initial registers (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Mai conducts a reading for Enid; Del steals a charm; Viv positions herself differently, careful and watchful.

• Topic sentence: In the play's opening, Pinnock installs the two daughters' coming-of-age arcs in their two initial registers — Del's hostile theft as the rebel's refusal, Viv's careful watchfulness as the studious mediator's attention, the second generation's two responses to the same household pressure already structurally distinct.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys foil-pairing inside Del's bitter declarative — Del's "I'm not even allowed to be black if it suits you" spoken as she pockets a charm from Mai's table, Viv silent and watchful beside her — combining foil-pairing (Del's hostile-rebel speech against Viv's careful-mediator silence, the two daughters of the same household responding to identical pressure with opposite first instincts) with the modal grammar of "allowed" (the verb converting maternal authority into permission-economics, Del's grievance arriving as the daughter's refusal to accept the conditional terms on which her inheritance is offered) inside the same opening setting.

• Close analysis: The simultaneity of Del's theft and Viv's watchfulness installs the two daughters as the two routes the diasporic Bildungsroman has had to invent — the daughter who refuses and the daughter who absorbs — both already practising the coming-of-age the play will track to its convergence.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the two arcs installed in two registers before either has developed — Del's body-to-body register, Viv's mind-to-canon register — both already operating inside the same room.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that coming-of-age in this play is doubled rather than single — and that the play's argument will be that both arcs are necessary.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Scene 1 to argue that the diasporic Bildungsroman has had to be doubled in 1980s Britain — that no single coming-of-age arc could carry the second generation's response to the host country's pressure, and that the two routes must converge for either to reach maturity.

• Context (AO3): The Black British literary movement of the 1980s — Caryl Phillips, Joan Riley, Pinnock, Buchi Emecheta — produced the diasporic Bildungsroman as a recognisable form, and Pinnock's two daughters are the dramatic dual-protagonist version of the same coming-of-age form.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 5): divergence under household pressure (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Del visibly pregnant has moved in; Viv visits with the missed English Literature exam between them; Del pushes Viv out.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed the two arcs as contrasting registers, Scene 5 stages their divergence under household pressure — Del pregnant and ousted from her biological mother's house, Viv missing the exam that was supposed to be the family's bet, the second generation's coming-of-age forced into a costly fork.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys protective stage business inside the off-stage event — Del's "You should not ruin your chances" eviction of Viv from Mai's bedsit, framed by the off-stage missed English Literature exam — combining protective stage business (Del's physical push as the older daughter's care delivered through structural separation rather than tenderness) with off-stage event (the exam kept off the stage like every other antagonistic British space, the institution's pressure registered in absence) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The modal "should not" is care delivered through structure — Del refusing to let Viv's coming-of-age be ruined by association with hers — and the off-stage exam stages the institutional pressure both daughters' arcs have had to absorb.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the divergence as the cost of the household pressure — Del's body-to-body arc taking her out of the biological mother's house, Viv's formal-educational arc forced to absorb the missed exam, both arcs now under visible strain.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the two routes have not been free choices — the household pressure has imposed the divergence, and the second generation's coming-of-age has had to operate inside conditions neither daughter chose.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Scene 5 to argue that the diasporic coming-of-age has had to absorb costs no British coming-of-age has had to carry — Del's pregnancy, Viv's missed exam, both arcs forced into divergence by the conditions the host country and the household together produced.

• Context (AO3): The Caribbean Education in Britain Working Party reported in 1971 that Caribbean-British students were placed in "educationally subnormal" schools at four times the rate of white peers; the same school-system pressure that produces Del's dyslexia mis-diagnosis is what produces the precarity of Viv's exam route in Scene 5.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): converged coming-of-age in two registers (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Del receives the handwritten book; Viv announces Black Studies at university; the two arcs converge on the same horizon.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 5 had stripped the two arcs to divergent paths, Scene 8 stages their convergence — Del's body-to-body arc completing in the cleaned bedsit with the handwritten book and the palm-reading, Viv's formal-educational arc completing in the Black Studies announcement, the second generation's doubled coming-of-age reaching its earned form.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural convergence inside doubled coming-of-age — Viv's "I'm going to study Black Studies" set alongside Del's silent receipt of Mai's handwritten book in the same Scene 8 cleaned bedsit — combining structural convergence (the academic and the body-to-body forms of the inheritance arriving as complementary rather than competing) with doubled coming-of-age (Viv's future-tense commitment and Del's wordless reception arriving as the two registers neither daughter could have invented alone) inside the same closing exchange.

• Close analysis: The naming of "Black Studies" converts Viv's assimilation route into reclamation, and the handwritten book registers Del's body-to-body alternative — the second generation's coming-of-age arriving in two registers because no single register could carry it, the convergence registered in the choreography of the closing scene rather than asserted in any one line.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the two arcs completing as the play's most quietly hopeful image — the second generation's doubled coming-of-age finally reaching the inheritance neither route alone could have carried.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the doubled coming-of-age as the play's structural answer — the diasporic Bildungsroman requires both daughters' arcs to converge before either can be said to have reached maturity.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be making her clearest argument about coming-of-age here — that the diasporic second generation cannot reach maturity through any single arc, and that the convergence of the two arcs is the form the Bildungsroman has had to take to survive in 1980s Britain.

• Context (AO3): The first British academic Centre for the Study of Race and Ethnicity emerged in the 1980s; the Black British literary and cultural studies movement Pinnock joined gave Caribbean-British students the formal-educational route Viv announces, and the body-to-body cultural inheritance Mai transfers to Del was the alternative the formal canon had refused — both routes necessary for the coming-of-age the host country had not made room for.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises coming-of-age as the doubled arc of two second-generation daughters — Scene 1 contrasting first registers, Scene 5 divergence under household pressure, Scene 8 converged coming-of-age in two complementary forms.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as two contrasting registers, Scene 5 stripped to divergent paths under crisis, Scene 8 confirmed as converged coming-of-age — three stages of the same diasporic Bildungsroman, the second generation reaching maturity only when both arcs reach the inheritance.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside the 1987 cultural moment in which the diasporic Bildungsroman was being established as a Black British literary form, Pinnock argues that coming-of-age in the diaspora has had to be doubled — and that the convergence is the form the genre had to take.

• Ultimate moral: Coming-of-age in Leave Taking is the doubled arc through which the diasporic second generation reaches the inheritance the host country had taught them to refuse — and the play's closing image is that doubled arc converged in one quiet scene.

T6 — Race and racism in 1980s Britain

Question

How does Pinnock present ideas about race and racism in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents race and racism at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses race and racism to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Race and racism in Leave Taking are the structural condition the household has been built inside — the casual workplace abuse Del is subjected to, the breakage of Caribbean-British men by housing discrimination and policing pressure (Gullyman, Viv's father, Brod), and the legislative chain that progressively eroded the Windrush generation's right to remain. Pinnock keeps the British institutions off-stage — the racist workplace, the police station, the Home Office — and stages instead the private household their pressure produces. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 Gullyman's racial abuse and Brod's Home Office threat, Scene 7 the truth about Viv's father broken by Britain, Scene 8 Viv's choice of Black Studies and the female line carrying what the racist host country could not break.

Technique gloss:

• Off-stage character — Gullyman, Viv's father, the racist workplace colleagues, the Home Office officer; the institutions of British racism kept off-stage so the household's interior bears their weight.

• Apposition (close analysis) — Brod's compound naming of the broken father.

• Stage business — Brod's loose tie and old clothes as silent characterisation of what British racism has cost the male Windrush figure.

• Doubled male-female outcome — racist host country breaking the male Windrush line (Gullyman, Viv's father, Brod) while the female line is forced to carry the inheritance.

• Structural reclamation — Viv's Scene 8 announcement of Black Studies as the academic response to a canon that had refused her inheritance.

• Recurring object — Mai's handwritten book of Obeah practice as the inherited form the racist host country had criminalised but could not erase.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): On 18 January 1981, thirteen young Black people died in the New Cross house fire in south-east London; on 10–12 April 1981, the Brixton uprising began in response to "Operation Swamp", the Metropolitan Police's stop-and-search campaign against Black men — and Pinnock wrote Leave Taking in 1987 inside the cultural moment those two events forced into public visibility.

• Building sentence (historical context): Pinnock places her household inside the legislative chain of the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, the 1971 Immigration Act, and the 1981 British Nationality Act — restrictions that progressively eroded the Windrush generation's right to remain — and stages the everyday racism of the 1980s through its consequences inside the family rather than its institutions outside it, the racist workplace, the police station, and the Home Office all kept off-stage so the household's interior bears their accumulated weight.

• Thesis: Across Scene 2 in which Brod's loose tie, Gullyman's racial abuse, and the Home Office threat to Brod's right to remain together register what 1980s Britain has cost the male Windrush figure, Scene 7 in which Brod's disclosure of the truth about Viv's father names the male Windrush outcome as a man "left broken and 'empty' by the way he was treated in Britain", and Scene 8 in which Viv's announcement of Black Studies at university and Del's reception of Mai's handwritten book together stage the female line claiming what the racist host country could not break, Pinnock presents race and racism as the structural condition inside which the diasporic family has been forced to carry its inheritance.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 2): Gullyman's racial abuse and Brod's Home Office threat (7 sentences — about 225 words)

Scene focus: Enid's living room; the pastor has visited; Brod arrives with his tie hanging loose and his clothes old; he tells Gullyman's story; the off-stage Home Office threat to his right to remain surfaces.

• Topic sentence: In the play's second scene, Pinnock establishes 1980s British racism not through any on-stage institution but through its consequences in the body of the male Windrush visitor — Brod's loose tie, Gullyman's off-stage breakdown after racial abuse, and the Home Office threat to Brod's right to remain together installing racism as the household's structural condition.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys the off-stage character function inside stage direction — Brod's Scene 2 retelling of Gullyman's story, "who went mad with grief after he was racially abused", framed by Brod's loose tie and old clothes and by the Home Office threat to his right to remain — combining off-stage character function (Gullyman as the off-stage extreme of the racist outcome, the Home Office officer as the off-stage institution producing it) with stage direction (the loose tie and old clothes as silent characterisation, the body of the Windrush man registering what decades of host-country racism have extracted) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The past participle "racially abused" names British racism as the agent and Gullyman as the recipient, while the verb "went mad with grief" registers the breakdown as the cumulative weight of the abuse rather than as Gullyman's individual fragility — the diagnosis returned to the structural cause.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears Brod's voice carrying the off-stage institutions of British racism into the household — the racist workplace that broke Gullyman, the Home Office that is now threatening Brod, the policing pressure that bracketed the play's 1980s setting.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that Pinnock declines to stage the institutions themselves — the racist colleagues, the Home Office officer, the police — and registers that the off-stage staging is itself the indictment, the household's interior bearing the weight British institutions refuse to.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 2 off-stage staging to argue that 1980s British racism produced its damage at the level of the household rather than at the level of the institutions that produced it, and that the diasporic family is the place where the cumulative cost of British racism has had to be absorbed.

• Context (AO3): The 1981 New Cross fire, the 1981 Brixton uprising, and the legislative chain of the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, the 1971 Immigration Act, and the 1981 British Nationality Act together formed the 1980s Britain inside which Pinnock wrote — and the Home Office threat that surfaces in Scene 2 anticipates by thirty-one years the 2018 Windrush deportation scandal the Guardian's reporting forced into public view.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Brod arrives drunk, complains about being denied rice and peas, and inside the disorder tells Del the buried truth about Viv's father.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Pinnock's diagnostic precision — "the way he was treated in Britain" names British racism as the structural cause without naming any single colleague or institution, the diagnosis returned to the country itself.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Del receives the handwritten book; Viv announces she has chosen to study Black Studies at university; the closing palm-reading stages the female line claiming the inheritance the racist host country had tried to erase.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 7 had named the male outcome of British racism, Scene 8 stages the female response — Viv's academic reclamation of Black Studies converging with Del's body-to-body reception of Mai's handwritten book, the female line claiming what the racist host country had broken the male line too far to carry.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural reclamation inside the cyclical structure — Viv's Scene 8 announcement of Black Studies at university framed alongside Del's receipt of the handwritten book in the same scene — combining structural reclamation (Viv's choice of Black Studies as the academic answer to a canon Pinnock herself has described as one in which she "could not find a trace of" her experience, the inheritance entering the academy through the daughter's choice rather than through the institution's permission) with cyclical structure (the same Mai's bedsit that opened the play "messy" now cleaned by Del, the inheritance returning to both daughters in two complementary forms) inside the same closing image.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the convergence as the play's quietly hopeful answer to the racist conditions Scene 2 and Scene 7 named — the female line claiming both the academic and the body-to-body forms of the inheritance the host country had refused.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that Pinnock's response to British racism is structural rather than declarative — the play does not stage the racist institutions in order to denounce them; it stages the female line claiming what those institutions had tried to break.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Viv's Scene 8 announcement and Del's reception together to argue that the diasporic answer to British racism is the female line claiming the inheritance the host country had refused, and that the claiming happens both in the academy (Viv's Black Studies) and in the bedsit (Del's handwritten book).

Callaloo" assembles the homeland in fragments because the homeland cannot now be returned to whole, and Scene 8 in which Del's reception of Mai's handwritten book and her palm-reading of Enid stage the only inheritance the migration could not erase, Pinnock dramatises memory and leave-taking as the controlling concept the play's title named — what the female line has had to carry forward because the homeland the older generation left has become unreachable.
