Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Enid arrives as Del's first client; the eighteen-year silence about her own marriage and her own mother in Jamaica finally cracks.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 7 had cracked the silence about Viv's father, Scene 8 cracks the silence about Enid's own marriage and her own mother — the older generation finally able to speak the suppressed truths inside the room the trained daughter has built.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys broken speech inside Enid's reproachful question — Enid's halting "You think life kind to me?" spoken to Del at Mai's cleaned bedsit during the closing palm-reading — combining broken speech (the interrogative refusing to assert, the eighteen-year silence breaking inside a question that asks the daughter to revise her prior reading of the mother) with the cultural-inheritance ritual (the palm-reading as the room inside which family truth becomes speakable, the inherited practice providing the holding space the host country had refused to provide) inside the same closing exchange.

• Close analysis: The fragmentation is the form of the truth's difficulty — a woman who has not spoken these sentences for eighteen years cannot now speak them whole — while the setting transfers the family's disclosure into Mai's cultural-inheritance space, registering that the truth can only be received in a room the older generation built and the younger generation has cleaned.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the Scene 8 fragmentation as the sound of a Windrush mother finally permitted, by her trained daughter, to articulate what eighteen years of host-country suppression had kept unsaid.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience receives Del's palm-reading of Enid as the play's quietest reversal — the daughter who once mocked Mai's practice now using it as the room inside which her mother can speak the family's silenced history.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Enid's confession to argue that family healing in the diasporic household requires the older generation's confession and the younger generation's reception in the same room — and that the silences the host country trained the family to keep can only be broken when the inherited practice has provided the holding space.

• Context (AO3): Leave Taking premiered at the Liverpool Playhouse Studio on 11 November 1987 and was revived at the Bush Theatre in 2018 with Sarah Niles as Enid — the revival opening weeks after the Guardian's reporting on the Windrush deportation scandal, Enid's broken-speech confession registering anew as the diasporic version of the public truth-telling the scandal forced.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises the family as a diasporic household built around silences the host country imposed — Scene 1 silences installed as the household's architecture, Scene 7 Brod's drunken disclosure cracking the first silence, Scene 8 Enid's broken-speech confession releasing the last.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as the household's silences, Scene 7 cracked through the trickster's drunken disclosure, Scene 8 released through the older generation's broken-speech confession — three stages of the same family truth-telling, the silences the host country had trained the household to keep finally speakable inside the inherited practice.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1987 about the diasporic family's silences and watching her play return to the Bush Theatre in 2018 inside the Windrush deportation scandal, Pinnock argues that family healing in the diaspora requires both the older generation's confession and the younger generation's reception in the same room.

• Ultimate moral: The family in Leave Taking is not the household its silences had pretended to be but the household its disclosures invent — and Mai's cleaned bedsit, with Del's reading hand on Enid's palm, is the diasporic family the play has spent eight scenes building.

T10 — Generational change and cross-generational inheritance

Question

How does Pinnock present the way inheritance passes between generations in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents the older generation

- how Pinnock presents the younger generation

- how Pinnock uses cross-generational inheritance to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Generational change in Leave Taking runs at the cross-generational-inheritance angle — NOT the 2025 older-younger conflict angle, which the ExactAngleRepeatPenalty rules out. The play's argument is for transmission rather than collision: what passes between the Windrush generation and the British-born second generation is the inheritance Pinnock has built the eight scenes to track. The plan follows three stages of the same transmission — Scene 1 the older generation's inheritance in three forms (patois, Obeah, food) installed alongside the younger generation's three refusals (silence, theft, dismissal), Scene 6 Mai's intervention as the older generation actively shaping the next, Scene 8 the inheritance transmitted in two complementary forms.

Required the model framing. Run the Q at "how inheritance passes between generations", not at "older-younger conflict". Frame the relationship as inheritance-transmission rather than as a quarrel (the quarrel angle is the 2025 angle, in the Coverage Pack as secondary).

Technique gloss:

• Cross-generational transmission — three forms (patois, Obeah, education) passing in three registers.

• Anagnorisis — Mai's "Del may have the gift of the Obeah" (Scene 7) as the older generation recognising the next.

• Recurring object — the handwritten book as transmission in material form.

• Doubled coming-of-age — both generations changing across the eight scenes.

• Cyclical structure — Mai's bedsit at open and close as the room of transmission.

• Stage business — Del's cleaning of the bedsit as the younger generation's preparation to receive.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Pinnock has framed the play in interview as the dramatisation of "the meeting between the African and European worldview that took place inside Caribbean culture" — and the meeting Pinnock stages between the older Caribbean-born generation and the British-born second generation is the diasporic continuation of that same encounter inside a North London household and a Deptford bedsit.

• Building sentence (historical context): Cross-generational inheritance in the diaspora has had to operate across the legislative chain that progressively eroded the older generation's right to remain (1962 CIA / 1971 IA / 1981 BNA) and the canon that refused the younger generation's experience — and Pinnock's play argues that transmission across these conditions is possible only when the older generation recognises the next and the younger generation cleans the room.

• Thesis: Across Scene 1 in which the older generation's three inheritance-forms (patois, Obeah, food) install alongside the younger generation's three refusals, Scene 6 in which Mai's "you must leave by Wednesday" deadline stages the older generation actively shaping the next, and Scene 8 in which the handwritten book and the Black Studies announcement complete the transmission in two complementary forms, Pinnock dramatises cross-generational inheritance as the active work both generations have had to do for the diasporic line to continue.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 1): three inheritance-forms, three refusals (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Mai's Deptford bedsit; Mai conducts a clairvoyant reading for Enid in patois; Del watches with hostile resistance and steals a charm.

• Topic sentence: In the play's opening, Pinnock places the older generation's three inheritance-forms — patois (Enid's register), Obeah practice (Mai's working life), the rooster's homeland-sound — inside the same room as the younger generation's three first refusals: silence, theft, dismissal.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys foil-pairing inside Enid's patois supplication — Enid's "that gel get her ways from you" spoken to Mai over the clairvoyant reading as Del silently pockets a charm — combining foil-pairing (three inheritance-forms in the older generation's register — patois, Obeah practice, the rooster-sound bedsit — against the younger generation's three refusals of silence, theft, dismissal) with the patois diagnostic of "get her ways from you" (Enid asking Mai to read the daughter's behaviour as inheritance, the line itself a transmission of the very inheritance Del's body is refusing) inside the same opening setting.

• Close analysis: The three forms and three refusals together register cross-generational inheritance as already in operation — the older generation transmitting in three registers, the younger generation receiving in body-level recognition even as her speech is refusing — and the play's question will be whether the registers can meet.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the inheritance in operation — patois on one side, silence on the other, the rooster's sound in between — before any character has named the transmission.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that cross-generational transmission has already begun even where it looks refused — Del's body's recognition of which object is worth taking is the first signal of the inheritance she will eventually claim.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Scene 1 to argue that cross-generational inheritance in the diaspora cannot be measured by what the younger generation says about it — the body's recognition precedes the speech's articulation, and the transmission is already in operation before either side has named it.

• Context (AO3): Pinnock said in 2018 that she wrote the play in part to depict "the black British experience as distinct from African American culture" — and the cross-generational inheritance of patois, Obeah, and food across the play's three generations is the dramatic embodiment of that distinct Caribbean-British inheritance.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 6): the older generation actively shaping the next (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Enid consults Mai about Del; Mai sets the deadline that Del must leave by Wednesday.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed the three inheritance-forms and three refusals as parallel registers, Scene 6 stages the older generation actively shaping the transmission — Mai not merely keeping the inheritance but deploying it to push Del toward the moment of reception.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys the catalyst function inside imperative — Mai's Scene 6 "you must leave by Wednesday" deadline set against Enid's consultation about Del — combining catalyst function (Mai actively producing the conditions inside which Del's reception becomes possible, the older generation engineering the transmission rather than passively keeping the inheritance) with imperative (the modal "must" delivering the deadline with the authority of the older practitioner) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The modal "must" registers Mai's role as cross-generational engineer rather than passive keeper — the older generation actively shaping the conditions inside which the next generation can come to the inheritance, the deadline structurally producing the Scene 7 kicked wall and Scene 8 cleaned bedsit.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels cross-generational inheritance as active work the older generation does rather than passive material the older generation keeps — Mai's deadline is the catalyst the next two scenes' transmission requires.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the older generation's role in cross-generational inheritance is not preservation but engineering — Mai shapes the conditions inside which the younger generation can come to the inheritance, and the engineering is the older generation's contribution.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Mai's Scene 6 deadline to argue that cross-generational inheritance requires the older generation to act, not just to keep — that transmission across the diasporic generations is achieved by engineering rather than by hope, and that the engineering is the dignity of the practitioner.

• Context (AO3): Pinnock has framed Obeah as "traces of African spiritual rituals preserved by clandestine practices" — practices that survived by being actively transmitted across enslavement, colonial criminalisation and migration — and Mai's Scene 6 deadline is exactly that active transmission staged in 1980s south London.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): transmission completed in two complementary forms (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Del receives the handwritten book; Viv announces Black Studies at university; cross-generational inheritance complete in two forms.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 6 had staged the older generation actively engineering the transmission, Scene 8 stages its completion — the inheritance arriving in the younger generation in two complementary forms, the body-to-body practice in Del's hands and the formal-educational study in Viv's announcement.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural completion inside Viv's declarative announcement — Viv's "I'm going to study Black Studies" paired with Del's silent reception of Mai's handwritten book in the same Scene 8 cleaned bedsit — combining structural completion (the cross-generational transmission arriving in two forms the older generation could not have specified in advance but the younger generation has invented) with cyclical structure (the unchanged room measuring the changed transmission, the future-tense "going to study" placing the inheritance into formal education even as the handwritten book places it into the body) inside the same closing image.

• Close analysis: The two forms of completion are the play's most subtle argument — the older generation kept the inheritance, the younger generation chose how to carry it forward, and the cross-generational work has been the coordination of those two choices — the inheritance neither imposed by the older generation nor invented by the younger generation but produced jointly across the eight scenes.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the completion as the cross-generational work earned — the older generation's keeping has met the younger generation's claiming, and the inheritance the legislative chain and the canon had between them tried to break is now in motion across the female line.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that cross-generational inheritance in the diaspora is jointly produced rather than singly transmitted — the older generation's engineering and the younger generation's invention together produce the form the inheritance can take in 1980s Britain.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be making her clearest argument about cross-generational inheritance here — that it cannot be transmitted unchanged across the diaspora, and that the joint work of the older and younger generations is the form the inheritance has had to take to survive the migration.

• Context (AO3): Stuart Hall's Cultural Identity and Diaspora (1990) names diasporic identity as "a matter of 'becoming' as well as of 'being'" — and Scene 8's two-form cross-generational completion is exactly that becoming, the inheritance the older generation kept arriving as a practice the younger generation has begun.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises cross-generational inheritance through three stages — Scene 1 three inheritance-forms set alongside three refusals, Scene 6 Mai actively engineering the transmission through the Wednesday deadline, Scene 8 transmission completed in two complementary forms.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as parallel registers of inheritance and refusal, Scene 6 named as the older generation actively engineering the transmission, Scene 8 confirmed as joint production across two complementary forms — three stages of the same cross-generational work, the inheritance the migration tried to break carried forward by both generations together.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside the 1987 cultural moment in which Black British identity was being formed for the first time, Pinnock argues that cross-generational inheritance in the diaspora is jointly produced rather than singly transmitted — and that the joint production is the form the inheritance has had to take.

• Ultimate moral: Cross-generational inheritance in Leave Taking is not the older generation handing what it kept to a younger generation that accepts; it is two generations working across the eight scenes to invent the form the inheritance can take inside 1980s Britain, and the closing image is that joint invention staged as Mai's withdrawal, Enid's confession, Del's reading hand, and Viv's announcement in one cleaned room.

Three context factors that travel across all 20 questions

Every essay should thread one of these into one BP. Pick the factor whose period-fit is sharpest for the Q.

1. The Windrush Era and the 1948 British Nationality Act. The MV Empire Windrush docked at Tilbury on 22 June 1948; the Windrush generation arrived 1948–1971 carrying British passports under the 1948 BNA. Most Caribbean migrants arrived on British-subject passports the 1962 Act retrospectively voided — that documentary erasure became the 2018 Windrush scandal. The 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act introduced employment vouchers; the 1971 Immigration Act made "patriality" the test for unrestricted entry; the 1981 British Nationality Act stripped the automatic citizenship the 1948 Act had granted. Best fit: C2 Enid, C4 Brod, C5 Female line, C6 Enid + Brod, C8 Enid + Del, T3 Mother-daughter, T6 Race / Racism, T7 Memory / Windrush legacy, T9 Family / household, CP1 Enid, CP2 Generational.

2. 1981 Britain — the cumulative hostile environment. The 1981 British Nationality Act passed in the same year as the Brixton uprising (10–12 April), the Toxteth riots (July), and the New Cross fire (18 January, in which thirteen young Black people died); these events together formed the cultural emergence of Black British identity as a distinct political and aesthetic category. Leave Taking premiered on 11 November 1987 inside that moment. Best fit: C1 Del, C9 Del + Viv, C10 Viv, T1 Identity, T2 Belonging, T5 Coming-of-age, T6 Race / Racism, CP2 Generational.

3. Obeah as suppressed Caribbean cosmology. Criminalised under the Jamaican Obeah Act 1898 (imprisonment with hard labour; the statute remains on the Jamaican books to this day); the practice survived through clandestine transmission across enslavement and migration. Pinnock has framed Obeah in interview as "traces of African spiritual rituals preserved by clandestine practices" — an alternative epistemology imperial Britain attempted to erase, and the inheritance the play centres. Best fit: C1 Del (reception arc), C3 Mai, C5 Female line, C7 Mai + Enid, T1 Identity, T4 Cultural inheritance, T7 Memory / Windrush legacy, T8 Religion, T10 Cross-generational inheritance.

Two critical voices that travel across all 20 questions

Every essay should name one critic, with year, in one BP or in the conclusion. Pick the voice whose claim is sharpest for the Q.

1. Stuart Hall, Cultural Identity and Diaspora (1990). Argues that diasporic identity is "a matter of 'becoming' as well as of 'being'" — not a fixed state to be reached but a practice to be performed, and that the Caribbean diaspora's identity has been formed by the same Atlantic the slave trade crossed and the same Atlantic the Windrush ship crossed. Best fit: C1 Del (becoming-as-arc), C2 Enid, C5 Female line, C8 Enid + Del, T1 Identity (the controlling concept), T2 Belonging, T3 Mother-daughter, T5 Coming-of-age, T7 Memory / Windrush legacy, T10 Cross-generational inheritance, CP1 Enid.

2. Lynette Goddard, Staging Black Feminisms (2007). Reads the centrality of women's experience in Black British theatre as the founding move of Pinnock's generation; argues that Pinnock and her contemporaries gave Black British women's lives theatrical representation for the first time. Best fit: C3 Mai, C5 Female line, C7 Mai + Enid, T3 Mother-daughter, T9 Family / household, C9 Del + Viv, C10 Viv, CP1 Enid, CP2 Generational.

For the religion / Obeah / cultural-inheritance questions specifically, Paul Gilroy's The Black Atlantic (1993) provides the strongest single AO3 anchor — his account of the inheritance of African religious practice carried through the Middle Passage describes exactly what Mai's handwritten book is. Best fit: T4 Cultural inheritance, T8 Religion, C3 Mai.
